choices.2: Use a mutex to protect shared data; use a semaphore to count or signal availability (a binary_semaphore can act mutex-like but a mutex has clearer ownership)
choices.3: They are interchangeable in all cases
choices.4: Always prefer a binary_semaphore over a mutex

question: What deadlock pattern does std::lock / std::scoped_lock prevent?

choices.1: Circular wait, where threads acquire the same mutexes in different orders and each holds one while blocking forever on another
choices.2: A thread locking the same mutex twice
choices.3: Priority inversion
choices.4: A thread forgetting to unlock

question: What is the core deadlock-avoidance strategy used to lock multiple mutexes?

choices.1: Use a single global lock for all mutexes
choices.2: Blockingly lock one mutex, try_lock the rest; if any try_lock fails, unlock everything and retry (often starting from the failed mutex)
choices.3: Sort all mutexes by address and lock in that fixed global order
choices.4: Lock them all simultaneously in hardware

question: Why does the retry strategy often start from the mutex that caused the failure?

choices.1: To impose a fixed global order
choices.2: Because try_lock cannot be called twice on the same mutex
choices.3: Because the first mutex is always the contended one
choices.4: To make progress: waiting blockingly on the contended mutex first avoids repeatedly failing the same try_lock in a wasteful loop

question: What does std::scoped_lock add on top of std::lock's deadlock-avoidance?

choices.1: RAII: automatic unlocking, exception safety, and cleaner syntax - the deadlock prevention itself comes from std::lock
choices.2: Support for recursive locking
choices.3: Timed locking
choices.4: A globally fixed lock order

question: What is a limitation of std::lock's deadlock prevention?

choices.1: It only works with exactly two mutexes
choices.2: It only prevents deadlock among the mutexes locked TOGETHER in one call; locking another mutex separately while holding them can still form a larger circular wait
choices.3: It requires a global mutex registry
choices.4: It cannot be used with RAII

question: Why is std::lock safer than a manual fixed lock order across a large codebase?

choices.1: A manual fixed order only works if EVERY site uses the same order (fragile); std::lock stays deadlock-free even when different code passes the mutexes in different orders
choices.2: It never blocks
choices.3: It is faster
choices.4: It sorts mutexes by name

question: What is the key behavioural difference between std::thread and std::jthread on destruction?

choices.1: std::thread calls std::terminate() if still joinable; std::jthread requests stop and then joins
choices.2: Both call std::terminate if joinable
choices.3: std::thread joins automatically; std::jthread calls terminate
choices.4: Both detach automatically

question: Does joinable() mean the thread is currently running?

choices.1: It means join() has already been called
choices.2: No - joinable means the object still represents a thread (owns a join/detach responsibility); the function may already have returned
choices.3: Yes, joinable means actively executing
choices.4: It means the thread has been detached

question: Why does a std::thread destroyed while still joinable (e.g. when an exception skips join()) call std::terminate?

choices.1: Because join() always throws
choices.2: Because the OS killed the process
choices.3: Because the thread leaked memory
choices.4: std::thread does not auto-join or auto-detach; a joinable thread at destruction is defined to call std::terminate() - use std::jthread or a scope guard for exception safety

question: What memory-visibility guarantee does a successful join() provide?

choices.1: The worker thread's completion synchronizes-with the return from join(), so writes the worker made are visible to the joining thread afterward
choices.2: It guarantees visibility even before join is called
choices.3: None; join is purely a wait
choices.4: It makes all the worker's variables atomic

question: How does std::jthread pass cancellation to its callable?

choices.1: If the callable's first parameter is a std::stop_token, jthread passes its token automatically; the worker checks st.stop_requested()
choices.2: It calls pthread_cancel
choices.3: It sets a global flag the callable must poll
choices.4: It throws an exception into the thread

question: Why are std::thread objects move-only (copy deleted)?

choices.1: Because two thread objects must not simultaneously own the same thread of execution; ownership is unique and transferable only by move
choices.2: Because copying would duplicate the OS thread
choices.3: To save memory
choices.4: Because threads cannot be stored in containers

question: What does std::thread::hardware_concurrency() return?

choices.1: The number of currently running threads
choices.2: The exact number of threads you must create
choices.3: The number of CPU sockets
choices.4: A hint for the number of hardware thread contexts; it may return 0 if not computable, so code should provide a fallback

question: What happens if an exception escapes the initial function of a std::thread or std::jthread?

choices.1: It detaches the thread
choices.2: It calls std::terminate(); exceptions are not auto-propagated, so catch inside the thread and convey via exception_ptr/promise
choices.3: It propagates to the joining thread
choices.4: It is silently swallowed

question: What is the danger of detaching a thread that captures local data by reference?

choices.1: Detached threads cannot access captured data at all
choices.2: The data becomes atomic automatically
choices.3: detach() throws if data is captured
choices.4: The detached thread runs independently and may use the data after it is destroyed (dangling); pass by value or via shared_ptr instead

question: What does a condition variable do, and what does it NOT store?

choices.1: It lets threads sleep until shared state may have changed; it does NOT store the condition itself - the condition lives in your shared data
choices.2: It stores the condition and the data
choices.3: It protects shared data like a mutex
choices.4: It queues notifications as permits

question: Why must cv.wait be used with a predicate (or a while loop), not a bare wait?

choices.1: For performance only
choices.2: Because a thread can experience spurious wakeups (wake with the condition still false); the predicate re-checks the actual state
choices.3: Because the predicate notifies other threads
choices.4: Because wait cannot take a lock otherwise

question: What is a 'lost wakeup', and how does putting the condition in shared state prevent it?

choices.1: A wakeup that wakes too many threads; use notify_one
choices.2: A thread that never calls notify
choices.3: A notification that fires before the consumer starts waiting (so a bare wait sleeps forever); a predicate over shared state lets the waiter notice the change and not sleep
choices.4: A spurious wakeup that returns early

question: Why must wait() atomically release the mutex and put the thread to sleep?

choices.1: To avoid holding the mutex too long
choices.2: Because the mutex must stay locked during sleep
choices.3: To make the predicate atomic
choices.4: To avoid a race where a notification arrives in the gap between deciding to wait and actually sleeping (which could otherwise be missed)

question: When should you use notify_one vs notify_all?

choices.1: Always notify_all for safety
choices.2: notify_one when only one thread can make progress (e.g. one item pushed); notify_all when multiple threads may proceed or during shutdown
choices.3: notify_one wakes everyone; notify_all wakes one
choices.4: They are identical

question: Does a condition variable remember a notify_one() if no thread is waiting?

choices.1: No - if no thread is waiting, the notification is simply lost; the real state must be stored separately (e.g. a bool)
choices.2: Yes, it queues all notifications
choices.3: Yes, the next waiter consumes it
choices.4: Yes, it stores it as a permit like a semaphore

question: When does notify_one() hand the mutex to the woken thread?

choices.1: Immediately; the woken thread resumes already holding the mutex
choices.2: Only if notify_all is used
choices.3: It does not - notify_one only wakes the thread; the woken thread must still compete to reacquire the mutex before wait() returns
choices.4: The mutex is never reacquired

question: What does a std::future<T> represent?

choices.1: A queue of tasks
choices.2: A mutex protecting a value
choices.3: A thread of execution
choices.4: A handle to a value that will become available later, connected to a shared state

question: What three things can a future's shared state hold?

choices.1: Only a return value
choices.2: A value, an exception, or a not-ready status
choices.3: A thread id, a mutex, and a counter
choices.4: A promise, a packaged_task, and an async handle

question: What does future.get() do?

choices.1: Starts the task and returns immediately
choices.2: Returns without blocking
choices.3: Waits until the result is ready, retrieves it (rethrowing a stored exception), and makes a std::future invalid
choices.4: Can be called any number of times on a std::future

question: How does std::shared_future differ from std::future?

choices.1: It is copyable and get() can be called multiple times (often returning a const reference), so many consumers can read the same result
choices.2: It cannot store exceptions
choices.3: It is move-only and single-use
choices.4: It runs the task eagerly

question: What is a 'broken promise' as observed through a future?

choices.1: A promise whose value was set twice
choices.2: If the promise is destroyed without setting a value or exception, the future becomes ready with a std::future_error
choices.3: A future that was never waited on
choices.4: A future copied without share()

question: What does std::async(std::launch::deferred, task) do?

choices.1: Does not run the task until get() or wait() is called, then runs it synchronously in the calling thread
choices.2: Runs the task immediately on a new thread
choices.3: Schedules the task on a thread pool
choices.4: Runs the task on the GPU

question: Why might std::async(task) (no launch policy) not start a new thread?

choices.1: It requires a packaged_task first
choices.2: Without an explicit policy the implementation may choose async OR deferred; use std::launch::async to force a new asynchronous task
choices.3: It never starts threads
choices.4: It always defers

question: What is the key difference between std::latch and std::barrier?

choices.1: A latch has phases; a barrier does not
choices.2: They are identical
choices.3: A latch is reusable; a barrier is single-use
choices.4: A latch is single-use (opens permanently at zero); a barrier is reusable and resets automatically after each phase

question: How does a std::latch work?

choices.1: It starts with a counter; count_down() decrements it, and threads in wait() proceed once the counter reaches zero (permanently)
choices.2: It counts up to a target
choices.3: It resets after reaching zero
choices.4: It blocks all threads until one calls open()

question: What does latch.arrive_and_wait() do?

choices.1: Only waits, without decrementing
choices.2: Decrements the counter and then waits - equivalent to count_down() followed by wait()
choices.3: Resets the latch
choices.4: Increments the counter

question: What does a std::barrier's completion function do?

choices.1: It runs once per phase, after all threads arrive but before they are released, useful for shared work done exactly once between phases
choices.2: It runs only when the barrier is destroyed
choices.3: It runs once per thread on arrival
choices.4: It cancels the barrier

question: What does barrier.arrive_and_drop() do?

choices.1: Drops the barrier entirely for all threads
choices.2: Resets the barrier to its initial count
choices.3: Arrives for the current phase but permanently reduces the expected arrival count for future phases
choices.4: Arrives and waits like arrive_and_wait

question: For repeated phase-based synchronization (e.g. finish phase 1 together, then phase 2 together), which tool fits?

choices.1: std::counting_semaphore
choices.2: std::barrier, because it is reusable across phases
choices.3: std::latch, because it can be reset
choices.4: A single std::future

question: What is the core difference between std::promise and std::packaged_task?

choices.1: packaged_task cannot capture exceptions
choices.2: promise wraps a callable; packaged_task does not
choices.3: Only promise produces a future
choices.4: A promise is a manual result provider (you call set_value/set_exception); a packaged_task wraps a callable so its return value/exception is captured automatically into the future

question: How does std::promise handle exceptions?

choices.1: It does NOT auto-catch; you must catch yourself and call p.set_exception(std::current_exception()), which is rethrown at future.get()
choices.2: It ignores them
choices.3: It catches them automatically
choices.4: It terminates the program

question: Conceptually, what does invoking a packaged_task do internally?

choices.1: Roughly: try { promise.set_value(callable(args...)); } catch (...) { promise.set_exception(std::current_exception()); }
choices.2: It only stores the callable for later, never running it
choices.3: It copies the result to every shared_future
choices.4: It blocks until a promise is set elsewhere

question: Why are std::promise and std::packaged_task move-only?

choices.1: Because each owns a unique shared state that must not be duplicated
choices.2: Because copying would deadlock
choices.3: To save memory
choices.4: Because they store raw threads

question: How many times can you normally call get_future() on a promise or packaged_task?

choices.1: Exactly twice
choices.2: As many times as you like
choices.3: Never; the future is returned automatically
choices.4: Once; calling it again throws std::future_error (use shared_future via .share() for multiple consumers)

question: Why is std::packaged_task especially useful for task queues and thread pools?

choices.1: It cannot be moved, so it stays in place
choices.2: It wraps a callable into a stored unit of work that can be scheduled and run later, delivering its result through a future
choices.3: It runs on the GPU
choices.4: It auto-creates threads

question: What are the four standard execution policies?

choices.1: sync, async, deferred, lazy
choices.2: read, write, shared, exclusive
choices.3: fast, slow, safe, unsafe
choices.4: seq (sequenced), par (parallel), par_unseq (parallel + vectorized), unseq (vectorized, single-thread)

question: What is the correct mental model for an execution policy like std::execution::par?

choices.1: A permission, not a command: the implementation MAY parallelize and may fall back to sequential execution (e.g. if resources are unavailable)
choices.2: A command that forces multi-threaded execution
choices.3: A guarantee of a specific thread count
choices.4: A vectorization-only hint

question: Under std::execution::par, who is responsible for avoiding data races (e.g. a lambda doing out.push_back)?

choices.1: The library handles all synchronization
choices.2: The caller - you must synchronize shared mutation (mutex/atomic) or use a reduction; par calls may run concurrently
choices.3: Data races are impossible under par
choices.4: par forbids any side effects at compile time

question: Why is using a mutex inside an unseq or par_unseq element-access function dangerous?

choices.1: Mutexes are slower under these policies
choices.2: Mutexes are not allowed to compile
choices.3: Calls may be unsequenced/interleaved within a thread, so blocking synchronization like a mutex can deadlock
choices.4: It forces sequential execution

question: What happens if an element-access function exits via an uncaught exception under a standard execution policy?

choices.1: The exception propagates to the caller normally
choices.2: The algorithm silently skips that element
choices.3: The policy downgrades to seq
choices.4: std::terminate is invoked (separately, std::bad_alloc may be thrown if temporary parallelization memory is unavailable)

question: Even under par, why shouldn't you assume iteration 0 runs before iteration 1?

choices.1: Because iterations always run in reverse
choices.2: Element order is not guaranteed; code that depends on iteration order (e.g. spin-waiting on another iteration) may hang
choices.3: Because par runs everything sequentially
choices.4: Because par sorts the range first

question: Which policy is best for a pure, independent, SIMD-friendly operation over large data, allowing both threads and vectorization?

choices.1: par_unseq
choices.2: unseq only
choices.3: No policy
choices.4: seq

question: What does std::async return and conceptually create?

choices.1: A mutex guarding the result
choices.2: A raw pointer to the result
choices.3: A std::thread you must join
choices.4: A std::future connected to a shared state holding the returned value, a thrown exception, or a deferred callable

question: What does the default std::async(f, args...) overload (no policy) mean for execution?

choices.1: It always defers
choices.2: It is equivalent to launch::async | launch::deferred, so the implementation may run eagerly on a thread OR defer until get()/wait()
choices.3: It always runs on a new thread
choices.4: It runs synchronously in place

question: With std::launch::deferred, when and where does the callable run?

choices.1: Immediately on a new thread
choices.2: In a library thread pool at construction
choices.3: Lazily, on the FIRST non-timed get()/wait(), in the thread that calls that waiting function
choices.4: It never runs

question: Why can `std::async(std::launch::async, []{ do_work(); });` (result not stored) behave as if synchronous?

choices.1: The returned future is a temporary destroyed at the end of the full expression, and a future from std::async can block in its destructor until the work completes
choices.2: async always runs synchronously
choices.3: The lambda is deferred
choices.4: do_work runs in the destructor

question: What should you use when you need GUARANTEED concurrent execution from std::async?

choices.1: Plain std::async(f) and hope
choices.2: std::async(std::launch::async, f), since the default policy may defer
choices.3: std::launch::deferred
choices.4: std::async with no arguments

question: How are exceptions from an async callable surfaced?

choices.1: They are stored in the shared state and rethrown when the caller invokes future.get()
choices.2: They terminate the program immediately
choices.3: They propagate on the worker thread to the OS
choices.4: They are logged and discarded

question: What must a C++20 coroutine 'task type' (e.g. Task<T>) provide?

choices.1: A mutex and condition variable
choices.2: A nested promise_type (plus a coroutine handle, result/exception storage, and typically an operator co_await and a continuation)
choices.3: Just a return statement
choices.4: A std::thread member

question: What does the 'continuation' stored in the promise enable?

choices.1: Running the coroutine on a GPU
choices.2: Detaching the coroutine
choices.3: Restarting the coroutine from the beginning
choices.4: Resuming the awaiting coroutine when this one finishes (symmetric transfer), so one coroutine can co_await another

question: Is a coroutine-based Task<T> automatically asynchronous (multi-threaded)?

choices.1: No - by itself it only suspends/resumes coroutines; real asynchrony requires connecting it to a scheduler, executor, thread pool, event loop, or async I/O
choices.2: Yes, the promise_type spawns a thread
choices.3: Only if T is void

question: How can a simple awaitable make a coroutine resume on a new thread?

choices.1: By calling std::async in await_ready
choices.2: Its await_suspend(h) launches a thread that calls h.resume() (e.g. std::thread([h]{ h.resume(); }).detach())
choices.3: By returning true from await_ready
choices.4: By storing the value in the promise

question: Why does Task<void> need a separate specialization from Task<T>?

choices.1: Because void cannot be stored in std::optional<T>, and it uses return_void() instead of return_value()
choices.2: Because void tasks cannot have a promise_type
choices.3: Because void coroutines cannot suspend
choices.4: Because exceptions can't be captured for void

question: What does the note recommend for real production coroutine code?

choices.1: Prefer a library task type (e.g. cppcoro::task, Folly coro::Task, Boost.Asio awaitable); rolling your own is mainly for learning
choices.2: Use std::async instead of any coroutine
choices.3: Always hand-roll your own Task type
choices.4: Avoid coroutines entirely

question: What defines a C++20 coroutine - what can it do that an ordinary function cannot?

choices.1: It cannot take arguments
choices.2: It can suspend its execution (via co_await/co_yield/co_return) and later resume from where it left off
choices.3: It can return multiple types
choices.4: It runs on a separate thread automatically

question: What do the three coroutine keywords do?

choices.1: They are interchangeable
choices.2: co_await and co_return both yield values
choices.3: co_await yields a value; co_yield ends; co_return waits
choices.4: co_await suspends until a result is ready; co_yield suspends and returns a value (generators); co_return ends the coroutine, optionally with a value

question: What is the role of the promise_type in a coroutine?

choices.1: It is the object responsible for managing the coroutine (e.g. its return object, suspend points, value/exception handling)
choices.2: It is the coroutine handle
choices.3: It stores a std::promise for the result
choices.4: It is the awaiter

question: Which three methods does an awaiter define to control how co_await behaves?

choices.1: await_ready(), await_suspend(), await_resume()
choices.2: initial_suspend(), final_suspend(), yield_value()
choices.3: lock(), wait(), unlock()
choices.4: begin(), next(), end()

question: Where is the coroutine frame/state stored in C++?

choices.1: On the heap (unlike some languages that keep it on the stack)
choices.2: In the promise object only
choices.3: Always on the calling thread's stack
choices.4: In a CPU register

question: In a generator promise_type, what does yield_value typically return and why?

choices.1: the value itself
choices.2: std::suspend_never, to keep producing without stopping
choices.3: a coroutine handle to the consumer
choices.4: std::suspend_always, so the coroutine suspends after storing each yielded value until the consumer resumes it

question: What are coroutines well-suited for?

choices.1: Guaranteeing real-time deadlines
choices.2: Asynchronous I/O, generators (lazy sequences), and cooperative multitasking
choices.3: Replacing all mutexes
choices.4: Forcing parallel execution across cores

question: What is std::generator (C++23)?

choices.1: An async I/O awaitable
choices.2: A container that eagerly stores all values
choices.3: A thread-pool task type
choices.4: A coroutine-based lazy range: the coroutine runs until co_yield, hands one value to the caller, suspends, and resumes on the next request

question: Why can a std::generator represent an infinite sequence safely?

choices.1: It runs on a background thread that you stop

choices.3: It precomputes a very large buffer

question: What is the big gotcha about std::generator being single-pass?

question: How do you yield every element of another range from a std::generator?

choices.1: co_yield std::ranges::elements_of(r)
choices.2: co_return the range
choices.3: return r.begin()
choices.4: co_await the range

question: What is a key limitation of std::generator regarding asynchrony?

choices.1: It cannot yield references
choices.2: It is synchronous - you cannot co_await an asynchronous operation inside a coroutine returning std::generator
choices.3: It must run on a separate thread

question: When is std::generator NOT the right tool?

choices.1: When you need random access, multiple passes, async co_await behavior, or maximum performance in hot inner loops
choices.2: When producing a sequence lazily
choices.3: When you want to avoid writing a custom iterator
choices.4: When composing with std::views::filter/transform

question: In a hand-written Generator<T>, which promise_type function makes co_yield work?

choices.1: get_return_object()

choices.3: return_value(T)
choices.4: initial_suspend()

question: In the iterator-based Generator, what does operator++ do?

choices.1: Copies the current value
choices.2: Destroys the coroutine

choices.4: Calls coro.resume() to advance to the next yielded value (rethrowing any stored exception)

question: How does the Generator's iterator detect the end of the sequence?

choices.2: By checking current_value is empty
choices.3: By a fixed length counter
choices.4: By catching an exception

question: What does this Generator's begin() do before returning an iterator?

choices.1: Nothing; it returns immediately
choices.2: It resumes the coroutine once to run up to the first co_yield (and rethrows any stored exception)
choices.3: It destroys the previous frame

question: What kind of generator is this hand-written Generator<T>?

choices.1: A lazy SYNCHRONOUS generator - it does not automatically wait for async I/O, timers, or background work
choices.2: A multi-threaded parallel generator
choices.3: An asynchronous generator that waits on I/O
choices.4: An eager generator that precomputes everything

question: What does a trailing const on a member function promise?

choices.1: That it won't modify the object's observable state; inside it, members are treated as const and only other const members may be called
choices.2: That the function is thread-local
choices.3: That the return value is const
choices.4: That it runs at compile time

question: What is the difference between low-level and top-level const for a pointer?

choices.1: Low-level const only applies to references
choices.2: Low-level const is what's pointed at (const int*) and matters for conversions/overloads; top-level const is the variable itself (int* const) and is ignored for parameter matching/copies
choices.3: Top-level affects the pointee; low-level affects the variable
choices.4: They are the same

question: What is the difference between bitwise const and logical const, and what bridges them?

choices.1: Logical const is enforced by the compiler; bitwise is a convention
choices.2: const_cast bridges them
choices.3: They are identical; nothing bridges them
choices.4: The compiler enforces bitwise const (the bits don't change); logical const means observable value doesn't change; the mutable keyword lets a specific member change inside a const function

question: Why is a std::mutex used inside a const getter typically declared mutable?

choices.1: Locking isn't 'logical' mutation; the convention is that const members are safe to call concurrently, so the mutex must be mutable to be locked within a const function
choices.2: To allow the getter to change the cached value
choices.3: To make the getter faster
choices.4: Because mutexes cannot be const

question: What convention links const member functions and thread-safety?

choices.1: const member functions should be safe to call concurrently (read-only implies thread-safe to share), which is why mutating through a const interface is dangerous
choices.2: const has no relationship to threading
choices.3: Only non-const members are thread-safe
choices.4: const members must never be called from multiple threads

question: Why should you avoid const-by-value return types (e.g. const T foo())?

choices.1: They are mostly pointless and block moves (the returned value can't be moved from)
choices.2: They prevent the function being constexpr
choices.3: They are a syntax error
choices.4: They force a heap allocation

question: What does std::as_const(x) do?

choices.1: Makes x constexpr
choices.2: Removes constness from x
choices.3: Deep-copies x as const
choices.4: Yields a const reference to x, forcing const access / the const overload (e.g. read-only iteration)

question: Why does the note say big-O alone misleads when choosing a container?

choices.1: Because allocation is free
choices.2: Because cache effects dominate: a cache miss (~100ns) vs L1 hit (~1ns) means contiguous containers (vector/array) with prefetching often beat pointer-chasing ones despite worse big-O
choices.3: Because big-O is always wrong
choices.4: Because all containers are O(1)

question: For a small key-value lookup (N <= ~32), what does the note recommend?

choices.1: std::list
choices.2: std::map for ordering
choices.3: std::unordered_map always
choices.4: A sorted vector + lower_bound, or even a plain linear scan - it often beats a hash map for small N

question: Why is 'use a list for lots of insertions' usually wrong?

choices.1: vector middle-insertion (a prefetched, bandwidth-bound memmove) often beats list insertion (O(1) but a cache miss plus an allocation per node)
choices.2: std::list doesn't support insertion
choices.3: list insertion is O(N)
choices.4: vector cannot insert in the middle

question: Which container does the note recommend for growing at BOTH ends?

choices.1: std::vector
choices.2: std::deque
choices.3: std::array
choices.4: std::list

question: For pure point-lookup throughput at scale, what is preferred over std::unordered_map?

choices.1: std::map
choices.2: std::list
choices.3: An open-addressing flat hash map (e.g. absl::flat_hash_map, boost::unordered_flat_map); use std::unordered_map only if you need its reference-stability guarantee
choices.4: A sorted vector

question: What is described as the single biggest performance win regardless of container choice?

choices.1: Using a custom comparator
choices.2: Always using std::map
choices.3: Disabling exceptions
choices.4: Avoiding repeated allocation - e.g. reserve(n) ahead of known sizes and reusing containers (clear() keeps capacity) on hot paths

question: What is std::array<T, N> and a key advantage over a raw C array?

choices.1: A view over existing memory
choices.2: A linked list of fixed size
choices.3: A heap-allocated dynamic array
choices.4: A zero-overhead fixed-size (size is part of the type) stack wrapper that has real value semantics: it copies/assigns and offers .size(), iterators, .at()

question: How is std::deque laid out, and what does that imply?

choices.1: A doubly-linked list of single elements
choices.2: A 'map' array of pointers to fixed-size chunks; it gives O(1) push at both ends but is NOT contiguous (no data()) and operator[] does two indirections
choices.3: One contiguous buffer like vector, so data() works
choices.4: A red-black tree

question: What stability guarantee does inserting/erasing at the ENDS of a std::deque provide?

choices.1: It invalidates everything
choices.2: It only invalidates the end iterator
choices.3: It does not invalidate references to other elements (a key difference from vector); middle insertion invalidates everything
choices.4: It invalidates pointers but not iterators

question: What are std::list's strengths and its main practical drawback?

choices.1: O(1) insert/erase given an iterator, O(1) splice, strongest iterator/reference stability - but cache-hostile (a random heap address and allocation per node), so it's almost always the wrong choice for performance
choices.2: Random access and great cache behaviour
choices.3: Compile-time fixed size
choices.4: Contiguous storage with O(1) indexing

question: What is std::priority_queue built on and what can you access?

choices.1: A red-black tree; you can iterate in order
choices.2: A binary heap over a vector: O(log N) push/pop, O(1) top, but no iteration and no random access (you only see the top)
choices.3: A hash table; O(1) lookup
choices.4: A doubly-linked list; O(1) splice

question: What is the default underlying container for std::stack and std::queue?

choices.1: std::deque
choices.2: std::vector
choices.3: std::array
choices.4: std::list

question: What is std::span<T> (C++20)?

choices.1: A bit-packed boolean array
choices.2: A non-owning view over a contiguous sequence - conceptually a pointer plus a length
choices.3: An owning dynamic array
choices.4: A hash table view

question: What problem does std::span solve as a function parameter?

choices.1: It copies the data for safety
choices.2: It deep-copies into a vector
choices.3: It makes functions run in parallel
choices.4: It gives one non-template signature that accepts vector, array, or C array, replacing error-prone (T* ptr, size_t n) pairs by carrying the length with it

question: What is the difference between span<T> and span<const T>?

choices.1: span<T> can modify the underlying elements (writes through); span<const T> is read-only - either way the span never resizes/reallocates
choices.2: span<const T> owns its data
choices.3: They are identical
choices.4: span<T> is read-only; span<const T> writes through

question: What does a static extent span (span<T, N>) provide over a dynamic-extent span?

choices.1: It owns N elements
choices.2: The size is baked into the type, enabling compile-time size checks and better codegen (unroll/vectorize), and it's smaller (just a pointer)
choices.3: It does bounds checking on []
choices.4: It can resize at runtime

question: What is the key lifetime caveat with std::span?

choices.1: It owns nothing, so it dangles if the backing container is destroyed or reallocated; use it for parameters/short-lived locals, not for storing/returning
choices.2: It cannot be passed to functions
choices.3: It always extends the lifetime of its backing data
choices.4: It copies its data on construction

question: How does slicing a span (first/last/subspan) work?

choices.1: It just adjusts pointer/length - nothing is copied
choices.2: It returns a vector
choices.3: It copies the selected elements into a new buffer
choices.4: It reallocates the backing container

question: How is std::map implemented and what does that give you?

choices.1: A linked list; O(1) splice
choices.2: A balanced BST (red-black tree): O(log N) guaranteed lookup/insert/erase, sorted iteration, lower_bound/range queries, and stable references
choices.3: A hash table; average O(1) unordered lookup
choices.4: A contiguous array; O(1) indexing

question: Why does std::unordered_map use separate chaining rather than faster open addressing?

choices.1: Because open addressing can't hash strings
choices.2: To support ordered iteration
choices.3: Because chaining is always faster
choices.4: Because the standard mandates reference stability, and node-based chaining keeps element nodes in place (only relinked on rehash)

question: What does a rehash in unordered_map invalidate?

choices.1: Iterators (the bucket array is rebuilt), but NOT pointers/references - the nodes are not moved, only relinked
choices.2: Only references, not iterators
choices.3: Both iterators and references
choices.4: Nothing

question: What triggers a rehash, and how do you avoid it during bulk inserts?

choices.1: load_factor exceeding max_load_factor (default 1.0); call reserve(n) to pre-size buckets
choices.2: Calling find(); use at() instead
choices.3: It happens on every insert and can't be avoided
choices.4: Erasing elements; call clear()

question: What happens to unordered_map lookups with a bad hash function (e.g. one that collides heavily)?

choices.1: Everything piles into one bucket and lookups degrade to O(N)
choices.2: It silently switches to a tree
choices.3: They stay O(1)
choices.4: The map refuses to compile

question: Why is operator[] dangerous for a read-only lookup (e.g. if (m[key] == 0))?

choices.1: It is slower than at()
choices.2: It throws if the key is absent
choices.3: It returns a copy, not a reference
choices.4: It silently INSERTS a default-constructed value when the key is absent (mutating the map); use find/contains for read-only checks

question: What is the difference between a vector's size and capacity?

choices.1: capacity is always twice the size
choices.2: size is how many elements currently exist (are constructed); capacity is how many the current allocation could hold before it must reallocate
choices.3: They are always equal
choices.4: size is the byte count; capacity is the element count

question: Why does geometric (multiplicative) growth make push_back amortized O(1)?

choices.1: Because push_back is always O(1) regardless of growth
choices.2: Because it preallocates infinite memory
choices.3: Because it never reallocates
choices.4: Multiplicative growth spreads the cost of relocations so N pushes total O(N); constant-increment growth would make N pushes O(N^2) from repeated copying

question: What does a vector reallocation invalidate?

choices.1: All iterators, pointers, and references into the vector - reserve up front to avoid it
choices.2: Only the end iterator
choices.3: Nothing
choices.4: Only references, not iterators

question: During reallocation, when does a vector MOVE elements vs COPY them, and why?

choices.1: It always moves for speed
choices.2: It moves only if the element's move constructor is noexcept, else it copies - for exception safety, so a throw mid-relocation can roll back to the intact original (std::move_if_noexcept)
choices.3: It moves only trivially-copyable types
choices.4: It always copies to be safe

question: What is the difference between push_back and emplace_back?

choices.1: emplace_back copies; push_back moves
choices.2: They are identical for all types
choices.3: push_back takes an already-constructed object (copy/move); emplace_back forwards its arguments to construct the element in place, avoiding a temporary
choices.4: emplace_back is always safer

question: Why is std::vector<bool> considered a trap?

choices.1: It cannot store false
choices.2: It is slower than vector<int> for all operations
choices.3: It is not part of the standard
choices.4: It is a bit-packed specialization: operator[] returns a proxy (not bool&), you can't take an element's address, and data() doesn't exist; use vector<char>/uint8_t or std::bitset instead

question: What does clear() do to a vector's capacity?

choices.1: It releases the allocation, setting capacity to 0
choices.2: It destroys all elements but keeps the allocation (capacity unchanged), so a cleared vector can be reused without reallocating
choices.3: It halves the capacity
choices.4: It calls shrink_to_fit automatically

question: What two problems with unscoped (C-style) enums does enum class fix?

choices.1: Inability to be used in switch statements
choices.2: Lack of a default value and missing iteration
choices.3: Slow lookup and large storage
choices.4: Name leakage into the enclosing scope, and implicit conversion to int - enum class scopes the names (E::Value) and forbids implicit int conversion

question: How do you convert an enum class value to its underlying integer?

choices.1: With a reinterpret_cast
choices.2: Explicitly via static_cast (or std::to_underlying in C++23) - there is no implicit conversion
choices.3: It converts implicitly
choices.4: By assigning it to an int directly

question: Why fix an enum's underlying type, e.g. enum class Side : std::uint8_t { Buy, Sell }?

choices.1: To enable implicit conversion
choices.2: To make the enum sortable
choices.3: For size control (1 byte vs 4), ABI/serialization stability, and to allow forward declaration
choices.4: To add iteration support

question: Why might you omit default in a switch over an enum?

choices.1: So the compiler (-Wswitch) warns about unhandled enumerators, turning 'add a new enum value' into a guided refactor
choices.2: To make the switch run faster
choices.3: To allow fallthrough between cases
choices.4: Because default is a syntax error for enums

question: For an enum class used as bit flags, why must you define operator| yourself?

choices.1: Because enum class can't hold flag values
choices.2: Because enum class has no implicit conversion to the underlying integer, so bitwise operators must be defined explicitly (cast to the underlying type and back)
choices.3: Because flags require a separate header
choices.4: Because operator| is deleted for all enums

question: Does std::vector require T's move constructor to be noexcept?

choices.1: No, but it strongly prefers a noexcept move so it can move (rather than copy) elements during reallocation
choices.2: Yes, it is a hard requirement
choices.3: No, and it never moves elements
choices.4: Yes, otherwise it won't compile

question: Why is moving with a potentially-throwing move constructor dangerous during reallocation?

choices.1: It corrupts the allocator
choices.2: If a move throws mid-relocation, some elements are moved-from and some untouched, and the original can't be restored - breaking the strong exception guarantee
choices.3: It is always slower than copying
choices.4: Moves can't be used in vectors at all

question: Why is copying safer than a throwing move during reallocation?

choices.1: Copying never throws
choices.2: Copying skips the old buffer entirely
choices.3: Copying is always faster
choices.4: Copying leaves the old elements untouched, so if a copy throws the vector can destroy the partial new buffer and keep using the original intact

question: What does std::move_if_noexcept express?

choices.1: Move the element if its move is noexcept (or it isn't copyable), otherwise copy - the logic vector uses to stay exception-safe
choices.2: Move only trivially-copyable types
choices.3: Never move; always copy
choices.4: Always move regardless of noexcept

question: What is the practical rule of thumb for types stored in a vector?

choices.1: Delete the move constructor
choices.2: Mark move operations noexcept (T(T&&) noexcept; T& operator=(T&&) noexcept;) whenever moving can't throw, so reallocation moves rather than copies
choices.3: Always provide only a copy constructor
choices.4: Make the move constructor explicit

question: In what sense are C++ exceptions 'zero-cost'?

choices.1: The normal/success path executes no extra instructions (no per-call flags); the cost is paid on the throw path and in optimisations the compiler must forgo
choices.2: They use no binary space
choices.3: Throwing is free
choices.4: They never flush the pipeline

question: What does the table-based (zero-cost) model emit off to the side instead of per-call checks?

choices.1: Unwind tables (.eh_frame / LSDA) describing cleanup/handlers per call site, plus out-of-line landing pads that run destructors
choices.2: Inline destructor calls on the hot path
choices.3: A try-block flag checked after every call
choices.4: A global exception counter

question: Why does the mere possibility of a throw inhibit compiler optimisation?

choices.1: It disables the optimiser entirely
choices.2: Each may-throw call adds an invisible control-flow edge to a landing pad, acting as a barrier that restricts hoisting/sinking/reordering/CSE/DSE across the call
choices.3: It doesn't; throws only cost at runtime
choices.4: It forces all functions to be inlined

question: Why does exception handling raise register pressure even when nothing throws?

choices.1: It reserves all registers for the unwinder
choices.2: It disables register allocation
choices.3: It adds a flag register per call
choices.4: Unwind-live objects (locals with non-trivial destructors) must be kept in a well-defined place - typically spilled to memory and kept live across calls - so the runtime can destroy them while unwinding

question: How does marking a function noexcept help the optimiser?

choices.1: It lets the compiler prove no unwinding: drop landing pads, reorder freely across the call, and pick faster library paths (e.g. vector move-on-grow)
choices.2: It forces tail-call optimisation
choices.3: It makes the function inline
choices.4: It removes all error handling

question: Why is the throw path itself a hardware worst case?

choices.1: The unwinder jumps to cold, unpredicted destructor/landing-pad code, so the branch predictor/return-stack buffer miss (guaranteed pipeline flush) and I-cache/I-TLB miss
choices.2: It disables the cache
choices.3: It blocks all other threads
choices.4: It runs twice as many instructions on the hot path

question: What are the four exception-safety guarantees from weakest to strongest?

choices.1: no guarantee, basic, strong, no-throw
choices.2: strong, basic, no-throw, no guarantee
choices.3: basic, strong, no-throw, no guarantee
choices.4: no-throw, strong, basic, no guarantee

question: What does the BASIC guarantee promise if an exception is thrown?

choices.1: The operation never throws
choices.2: The operation has no observable effect
choices.3: The object is destroyed
choices.4: No resources leak, invariants are preserved, and objects remain valid - but state may be partially changed

question: What does the STRONG guarantee promise, and what pattern implements it?

choices.1: That destructors run
choices.2: Commit-or-rollback: on throw the operation has no observable effect; commonly via 'make a temporary copy, modify it, then swap'
choices.3: That the function never throws; mark it noexcept
choices.4: That state may be partially changed but valid

question: What is the no-throw guarantee, and which operations typically aim for it?

choices.1: The operation rolls back on throw; constructors
choices.2: The operation has basic safety; all member functions
choices.3: The operation may throw but won't leak; getters
choices.4: The operation is guaranteed not to throw at all; destructors (ideally), swap for some types, and move operations marked noexcept

question: What level does a lot of well-written library code aim for at minimum (and often stronger for modifying operations)?

choices.1: At least the basic guarantee, and often the strong guarantee for modifying operations when practical
choices.2: No guarantee
choices.3: Only the no-throw guarantee
choices.4: None; exceptions are avoided entirely

question: Which fstream types are used for reading vs writing vs both?

choices.2: They are interchangeable
choices.3: ofstream reads, ifstream writes, fstream neither
choices.4: ifstream reads from a file, ofstream writes to a file, fstream does both

question: By default, what does opening a file with std::ofstream do to an existing file, and how do you append instead?

question: What is the recommended safety check after opening a file?

question: Which flag combination opens a file for both reading and writing while appending?

question: What is the core type of the <filesystem> library (C++17)?

choices.1: std::filesystem::path, representing a filesystem path

question: How do most filesystem functions report errors, and what is the non-throwing alternative?

question: What is a stream in C++?

choices.4: A fixed-size buffer

question: Why does mixing std::cin >> x followed by std::getline often misbehave?

question: After a failed extraction (e.g. non-numeric input to >> int), what two steps recover the stream?

question: What is the difference between int f; and int f{}; at block scope?

question: What safety property does brace {} initialization add over = or () init?

question: Why does std::vector<int> v{3, 0} differ from std::vector<int> v(3, 0)?

question: What is an aggregate that can be brace-initialized member-by-member?

question: What do C++20 designated initializers allow, with what restriction?

choices.4: Initializing private members directly

choices.2: A narrowing conversion error
choices.3: Anything that could be parsed as a function declaration is (e.g. Widget w(); declares a function); Widget w{}; instead declares a value-initialized object

question: In a structured binding like `auto& [a, b] = p;`, what exactly does the `&` (and the introducer's qualifiers in general) apply to?

question: Iterating a `std::map<std::string, int>` with `for (auto& [name, score] : scores)`, you can do `score += 1` but cannot reassign `name`. Why?

choices.3: Because a map's element type is `std::pair<const K, V>`, so the key member is const and the value member is mutable; the bindings inherit those member qualifiers
choices.4: Because structured bindings always make the first name read-only and the rest writable

question: When a structured binding decomposes a struct/aggregate, which members does it bind to, and in what order?

choices.1: All data members including private and static ones, in alphabetical order
choices.2: The public non-static data members, in their declaration order
choices.3: Only members that have a default member initializer, in reverse declaration order
choices.4: Whichever members are returned by the type's `get<>()` overloads

question: Which of the following is NOT one of the three categories that structured bindings can decompose, per the note?

choices.1: Built-in arrays such as `int arr[3]`
choices.2: Tuple-like types (`pair`, `tuple`, `array`, or anything providing `tuple_size`/`get`)
choices.3: Structs/aggregates, binding to their public non-static data members in order
choices.4: A raw pointer, which is decomposed into its address and the pointee

question: What is the purpose of writing `auto&& [a, b] = make_pair(...);` rather than `auto [a, b] = ...`?

choices.1: Forwarding/universal-reference binding — it binds to the temporary without making a copy of it
choices.2: It deep-copies the temporary twice to guarantee exception safety
choices.3: It forces both `a` and `b` to be const, preventing any modification
choices.4: It is required syntax whenever the right-hand side is a function call

question: How does `std::tie(x, y) = std::make_pair(1, 2);` differ from `auto [x, y] = std::make_pair(1, 2);`?

choices.1: `std::tie` works only on `std::array`, while structured bindings work only on `std::pair`
choices.2: `std::tie` assigns into pre-existing variables, whereas a structured binding declares brand-new names in that statement
choices.3: `std::tie` always binds by reference while a structured binding always copies, with no way to change either
choices.4: There is no difference; `std::tie` is just the C++17 spelling of a structured binding

question: Why is `if (auto [it, inserted] = m.try_emplace(key, value); inserted) { ... }` a clean idiom?

choices.1: Because `try_emplace` returns a `pair<iterator, bool>`, and the if-init-statement scopes that lookup tightly to the branch while letting you test `inserted` directly
choices.2: Because the init-statement makes the structured binding `constexpr`, evaluating the insertion at compile time
choices.3: Because `try_emplace` returns a `tuple<iterator, bool, size_t>` that only an if-statement can unpack
choices.4: Because structured bindings are only legal inside `if`/`switch` init-statements

question: Which statement about pre-C++26 limitations of structured bindings is correct?

choices.1: You may name fewer variables than there are elements; the trailing elements are silently discarded
choices.2: You must name every element (no placeholder to skip one before C++26's `_`), and you cannot make the declaration `constexpr`
choices.3: You can mix references and values per name as long as the declaration is `constexpr`
choices.4: You can decompose only types whose element count is known at runtime

question: In `auto [a, b] = p;`, what does the `[a, b]` syntax denote?

choices.1: A lambda capture list that captures `a` and `b` by value
choices.2: An array subscript selecting elements `a` and `b` of `p`
choices.3: The structured-binding introducer that names the decomposed elements — it is not a lambda capture
choices.4: An attribute specifier applied to the declaration

question: What constraint governs the number of names in a structured binding declaration?

choices.1: There must be at least one name; extras beyond the element count are default-initialized
choices.2: The number of names must exactly match the number of elements/members being decomposed
choices.3: The number of names may be any value; unmatched names become uninitialized
choices.4: The count must be a power of two for tuple-like types

question: Given `std::pair<int, std::string> p{1, "hi"}; const auto& [id, name] = p;`, which description is accurate?

choices.1: `id` and `name` are read-only references that alias `p`'s members without copying them
choices.2: `id` and `name` are independent copies of `p`'s members that may still be modified
choices.3: `p` is moved-from, leaving `id` and `name` as the only valid handles to the data
choices.4: Only `name` is a reference; `id` is copied because `int` is a scalar

question: Why can you not call std::sort on a std::list, forcing the list to provide its own member .sort()?

choices.1: std::sort cannot relink nodes, and list elements must be relinked rather than swapped
choices.2: std::sort is not declared as a friend of std::list, so it cannot access the nodes
choices.3: std::sort requires random-access iterators (it jumps around while partitioning), but list's iterators are only bidirectional
choices.4: std::sort requires contiguous iterators so it can memcpy elements, which a list cannot provide

question: std::lower_bound compiles for any forward iterator. Why is it genuinely O(log N) on a std::vector but effectively O(N) on a std::list?

choices.1: Because lower_bound performs O(log N) comparisons either way, but advancing a bidirectional iterator to the midpoint is O(N) on a list and O(1) on a vector
choices.2: Because list iterators cannot be compared with <, so lower_bound falls back to a linear scan
choices.3: Because list stores no size, so lower_bound must first count the elements in O(N)
choices.4: Because vector caches previous search results while list does not

question: What does end() refer to, and how is the range [begin, end) characterised?

choices.1: end() points one past the last element and is a non-dereferenceable sentinel; the range is half-open, and an empty range is begin == end
choices.2: end() points at the first element; iteration runs backwards to begin()
choices.3: end() points at the last element; the range is closed on both sides
choices.4: end() points at a copy of the last element used only for bounds checking

question: What is the correct weak-to-strong ordering of the iterator category hierarchy?

choices.1: forward -> input/output -> bidirectional -> contiguous -> random-access
choices.2: input/output -> bidirectional -> forward -> random-access -> contiguous
choices.3: input/output -> forward -> bidirectional -> random-access -> contiguous
choices.4: random-access -> contiguous -> bidirectional -> forward -> input/output

question: What does the contiguous iterator category (C++17) guarantee beyond random-access?

choices.1: That dereferencing is O(1) instead of O(log N)
choices.2: That elements are physically adjacent in memory, so &*it yields a real pointer (std::to_address works) and the data is memcpy-able / SIMD- and C-passable
choices.3: That the iterator can move both forwards and backwards
choices.4: That the container will never invalidate the iterator

question: Which is the weakest iterator category that std::find requires?

choices.1: Input
choices.2: Bidirectional
choices.3: Random-access
choices.4: Contiguous

question: What is the role of std::iterator_traits in generic code, as used by std::distance / std::advance?

choices.1: It guarantees the iterator is never invalidated during the algorithm
choices.2: It exposes value_type, difference_type, reference, pointer and iterator_category so algorithms can dispatch on the category and pick the optimal implementation
choices.3: It rebinds the iterator's allocator to the algorithm's needs
choices.4: It converts any iterator into a random-access iterator at compile time

question: When writing a custom iterator, which category should you advertise?

choices.1: Whichever category matches the value_type's size
choices.2: Always forward, because it is the safest default for any container
choices.3: The weakest category your structure can honestly support in the stated complexity; claiming random-access you can't deliver in O(1) breaks algorithms' complexity guarantees
choices.4: Always random-access, since it enables the most algorithms

question: What does assigning through a std::back_inserter adaptor do?

choices.1: It overwrites the element the underlying iterator currently points at
choices.2: It calls push_back on the underlying container, growing it as you copy
choices.3: It reverses the direction of iteration
choices.4: It moves the element out of the source, leaving it empty

question: What is a lambda, fundamentally?

choices.1: A compiler-generated closure struct with an operator(); captured variables become its data members
choices.2: A special stack-allocated function pointer with no state
choices.3: A macro that the preprocessor expands into a free function
choices.4: A runtime object allocated on the heap and managed by std::function

question: By default, can a lambda modify variables it captured by value inside its body, and what changes that?

choices.1: No, because operator() is const by default; adding mutable removes the const so the body can modify its own copies
choices.2: No, and there is no way to allow it short of capturing by reference
choices.3: Yes by default; const removes the ability
choices.4: Yes, and the modification is reflected in the original outer variable

question: At what moment is a by-value capture's value fixed?

choices.1: When the lambda is created — the value is copied at that point, so later changes to the outer variable are not seen
choices.2: When the enclosing scope exits
choices.3: It is never fixed; by-value captures always alias the original
choices.4: When the lambda is invoked (it re-reads the variable each call)

question: Inside a member function, what does [this] actually capture, and what is the safe way to snapshot the whole object?

choices.1: It captures a reference to *this that is automatically extended to the lambda's lifetime
choices.2: It captures nothing; member access inside a lambda is illegal
choices.3: It copies all the object's members; no snapshot mechanism is needed
choices.4: It captures the object pointer (members are accessed through it, so it dangles if the object dies); [*this] (C++17) stores an independent copy of the object

question: Why is [=] inside a member function a notorious surprise?

choices.1: It silently converts the lambda into a function pointer
choices.2: It deep-copies every member including base subobjects, which is very expensive
choices.3: It also captures the this pointer (not copies of the members), so member access still dangles if the object is destroyed
choices.4: It captures nothing at all, despite appearances

question: A function returns a lambda that captured a local by reference ([&local]); the returned lambda is later invoked. What is the rule and fix?

choices.1: It is UB because local is destroyed when the function returns; capture by value/move anything that must outlive the enclosing scope
choices.2: It is fine because the compiler extends local's lifetime to match the lambda
choices.3: It is UB only if local is larger than a cache line
choices.4: It is fine because references are reference-counted

question: What problem does init-capture (e.g. [ptr = std::move(p)]) solve?

choices.1: It enables capturing by move (e.g. a unique_ptr) and capturing a computed value, by creating a new closure member with an initializer
choices.2: It converts a capturing lambda into a function pointer
choices.3: It allows one binding to be a reference and another a value
choices.4: It makes the lambda implicitly constexpr

question: Which lambdas are convertible to a plain function pointer, and why?

choices.1: Only generic (auto-parameter) lambdas
choices.2: Only mutable lambdas, because they expose their state
choices.3: Only capture-less (stateless) lambdas, because a capturing lambda carries state that a function pointer cannot hold
choices.4: Any lambda, because operator() is always static

question: With auto f = [x]() mutable { return ++x; }; called twice, what is observed?

choices.1: f() returns 1 then 2 (state persists in the closure's copy), while the outer x is unchanged
choices.2: It fails to compile because ++ cannot apply to a captured value
choices.3: f() returns 1 then 2, and the outer x becomes 2 as well
choices.4: f() returns 1 then 1, because the copy is reset each call

question: What is the overarching principle for hot-path code in this note?

choices.1: Inline every function to remove all call overhead
choices.2: Always prefer branchless code over branchy code
choices.3: Do no work that isn't essential: no allocation, no virtual dispatch, no exceptions, no cache misses, no mispredicted branches
choices.4: Use as many threads as cores to maximise throughput

question: What does [[likely]]/[[unlikely]] (or __builtin_expect) achieve?

choices.1: It forces the branch predictor to always predict taken
choices.2: It steers code layout so the common branch falls through (no taken-branch penalty) and the cold path is moved out of line
choices.3: It pins the branch to a specific CPU core
choices.4: It removes the branch entirely, producing branchless code

question: What does __restrict promise the compiler, and what is the danger?

choices.1: That the pointed-to memory is read-only for the function's duration
choices.2: That memory is cache-aligned, enabling wider loads
choices.3: That a pointer is never null, enabling null-check elimination
choices.4: That two pointers don't alias, letting the compiler keep values in registers and vectorize — but it is UB if the pointers actually do alias

question: Why prefer static dispatch (templates/CRTP, variant + visit) over virtual calls in an inner loop?

choices.1: Virtual functions cannot be marked noexcept
choices.2: Virtual calls always throw exceptions on the hot path
choices.3: Virtual calls allocate memory on every call
choices.4: A virtual call is an indirect branch (often mispredicted) and also blocks inlining across the call boundary

question: How does the note characterise the cost of exceptions on the hot path?

choices.1: They have no performance impact in optimised builds
choices.2: They are always expensive, so the hot path should catch them eagerly
choices.3: They are cheaper than return codes, so prefer them everywhere
choices.4: They are zero-cost when not thrown, but a throw is very expensive and the machinery can inhibit some optimizations — keep the hot path exception-free and validate at the boundary

question: Which of these is called out as a source of hidden allocations to watch for on the hot path?

choices.1: constexpr variables
choices.2: string_view and span
choices.3: std::array and stack buffers
choices.4: std::function (may allocate for large callables), std::string past SSO, std::stringstream, and lambda-to-function conversions

question: What is the recommended remedy for false sharing of hot per-thread data?

choices.1: Make the data const so threads cannot write it
choices.2: Disable the hardware prefetcher
choices.3: Align/pad the data to a cache line (e.g. alignas(std::hardware_destructive_interference_size)) so independent fields sit on different lines
choices.4: Store the data in a single global atomic

question: What is the purpose of std::unreachable() / [[assume(cond)]] on the hot path?

choices.1: They force the enclosing function to be inlined
choices.2: They give the optimizer facts (impossible paths / invariants) so it can prune branches and bounds work it would otherwise keep
choices.3: They emit a runtime trap if the condition is violated, for debugging
choices.4: They mark the branch as unlikely for layout purposes

question: What is the note's caution about branchless code?

choices.1: Branchless code cannot be vectorized
choices.2: It is always faster, so it should be the default
choices.3: Branchless code is undefined behaviour on x86
choices.4: Predictable branches are essentially free and branchless code can be slower; reserve it for unpredictable data-dependent branches and measure

question: Why does the compiler insert padding between struct members?

choices.1: To place each member at an address that satisfies its alignment requirement
choices.2: To store hidden type information for RTTI
choices.3: To make the struct size a power of two
choices.4: To reserve space for future members

question: To minimise wasted space from padding, how should you order a struct's fields?

choices.1: By how frequently each field is accessed
choices.2: By increasing alignment requirement (smallest first)
choices.3: Alphabetically by member name
choices.4: By decreasing alignment requirement (largest-aligned members first)

question: For struct Example { char c; int i; double d; }, what is sizeof(Example) and why?

choices.1: 24, because each member is rounded up to 8 bytes
choices.2: 12, because char and int pack into the first 8 bytes
choices.3: 16, because of padding after c and after i, with the struct padded up to the largest member's 8-byte alignment
choices.4: 13, because that is the exact sum of the member sizes

question: Why is the struct itself padded at the end to a multiple of its largest member's alignment?

choices.1: To allow memset to zero it in one instruction
choices.2: So that in an array of the struct, every element still starts at a properly aligned address
choices.3: To match the cache line size exactly
choices.4: To leave room for a vtable pointer

question: How do you stop two threads' independent hot counters from causing false sharing?

choices.1: Declare them thread_local in the same struct
choices.2: Mark both counters volatile
choices.3: Combine them into one atomic variable
choices.4: Put each on its own cache line using alignas(64) (or std::hardware_destructive_interference_size)

question: What is the cost when a misaligned double straddles an alignment boundary?

choices.1: The compiler refuses to compile the program
choices.2: It is always faster because it skips alignment checks
choices.3: The value is silently truncated to fit
choices.4: The CPU may need two memory accesses instead of one, and some architectures fault on the misaligned access entirely

question: When is removing padding via #pragma pack(1) / __attribute__((packed)) appropriate, per the note?

choices.1: Always, since smaller structs are universally faster
choices.2: Whenever a struct exceeds 64 bytes
choices.3: Only for I/O / serialization layouts — not hot data — because misaligned access can be slower or even fault
choices.4: For hot, compute-heavy data structures, to save cache

question: When you access one field across many objects, why can Structure-of-Arrays (SoA) beat Array-of-Structs (AoS)?

choices.1: SoA packs each field contiguously, giving better cache utilization and SIMD-friendliness and avoiding dragging unused fields into cache
choices.2: SoA uses less total memory because it has no padding at all
choices.3: SoA guarantees alignment to 64 bytes automatically
choices.4: AoS cannot be iterated with range-based for

question: Which tools/operators does the note suggest to confirm a struct's actual layout?

choices.1: valgrind and gdb breakpoints
choices.2: strace and ltrace
choices.3: nm and objdump symbol tables
choices.4: sizeof / alignof, plus pahole or clang -fdump-record-layouts

question: What is the minimal modern Allocator interface a container needs?

choices.1: A single allocate function returning constructed objects
choices.2: Only operator new and operator delete
choices.3: construct, destroy, max_size and rebind<U>::other
choices.4: value_type, allocate, deallocate and operator==, plus a templated converting (rebind) constructor

question: What does allocate(n) return, and what constructs the elements?

choices.1: Raw, uninitialized storage for n objects; the container constructs and destroys the elements separately (allocation is decoupled from object lifetime)
choices.2: Fully constructed objects ready to use
choices.3: A reference-counted block that auto-destroys on deallocate
choices.4: Zero-initialized objects that still need their constructors run

question: Why does the rebind (templated converting) constructor matter for a std::list?

choices.1: Because rebind disables exceptions during allocation
choices.2: Because list allocates internal node objects, not T, so the allocator must be rebindable to MyAllocator<Node>
choices.3: Because list needs to allocate T plus a reference count
choices.4: Because list stores elements contiguously and needs alignment metadata

question: What does allocator equality (operator==) decide?

choices.1: Whether the two allocators were constructed in the same translation unit
choices.2: Whether the allocator supports the rebind constructor
choices.3: Whether the allocator is thread-safe
choices.4: Whether memory from one allocator instance can be freed by another (interchangeability), which governs cheap move/swap of containers

question: Why do containers go through std::allocator_traits rather than calling the allocator directly?

choices.1: Because the allocator's allocate method is private
choices.2: Because allocator_traits supplies sensible defaults (construct/destroy, rebind, max_size, propagation traits) so a minimal allocator just works
choices.3: Because allocator_traits is required to allocate on the stack
choices.4: To bypass the allocator's operator== for speed

question: In a pool/free-list allocator, what does the union Slot { T value; Slot* next; } trick accomplish?

choices.1: It allows the pool to serve allocations of arbitrary size
choices.2: It guarantees the object is always cache-line aligned
choices.3: It stores the free-list pointer inside the slot while it is unused, so the pool needs zero extra metadata, with O(1) lock-free allocate/deallocate
choices.4: It lets a slot hold both an object and its free-list link simultaneously

question: How does an arena / bump allocator work, and what standard resource embodies it?

choices.1: It frees each object individually via a free list; std::pmr::synchronized_pool_resource
choices.2: It coalesces adjacent free blocks on every free; std::pmr::unsynchronized_pool_resource
choices.3: It allocates by advancing a pointer and frees everything at once by resetting the pointer; std::pmr::monotonic_buffer_resource
choices.4: It uses reference counting to reclaim memory; std::pmr::new_delete_resource

question: Why do custom pool/arena allocators help latency versus the global new/delete?

choices.1: They guarantee allocations never fail
choices.2: They run constructors in parallel
choices.3: No lock, no syscall/page fault on the hot path, better cache locality, and deterministic O(1) with no tail-latency spikes
choices.4: They compress objects to use less memory

question: What is the pragmatic advantage of PMR (polymorphic_allocator + memory resources) over hand-rolled allocators?

choices.1: It gives arena/pool behaviour without templating the container on the allocator type, so containers of different resources share one type
choices.2: It is always faster than any hand-rolled allocator
choices.3: It eliminates the need for allocate/deallocate entirely
choices.4: It makes all allocations thread-safe by default

question: Is malloc a system call, and how does it usually satisfy a request?

choices.1: Yes, but only the first malloc call ever touches the kernel
choices.2: Yes, every malloc traps into the kernel to get fresh pages
choices.3: No — it is a library function; a user-space allocator (ptmalloc/tcmalloc/jemalloc) manages heaps obtained from the OS and sub-allocates chunks, usually with no syscall on the fast path
choices.4: No — malloc is a compiler intrinsic resolved at compile time

question: How does glibc malloc typically satisfy small/medium versus large allocations?

choices.1: Both via mmap, differing only in flags
choices.2: Small via mmap, large via sbrk
choices.3: Small/medium by extending the heap with sbrk/brk; large (roughly > 128 KiB) via a separate mmap region that can be individually returned with munmap
choices.4: Both via sbrk only

question: How do new and malloc differ on allocation failure?

choices.1: Both throw std::bad_alloc
choices.2: new throws std::bad_alloc, whereas malloc returns NULL
choices.3: Both return NULL
choices.4: new returns NULL, whereas malloc throws std::bad_alloc

question: What two actions does the new expression perform?

choices.1: It only allocates memory; the constructor must be called manually
choices.2: It calls operator new to obtain raw memory and then runs the object's constructor in that memory (placement-new internally)
choices.3: It runs the constructor first, then allocates memory to hold the result
choices.4: It calls malloc and then memsets the memory to zero

question: If a constructor throws during evaluation of a new expression, what happens to the allocated memory?

choices.1: It is zeroed and returned to the caller anyway
choices.2: It becomes undefined-behaviour territory the runtime cannot handle
choices.3: It is automatically freed via the matching operator delete
choices.4: It leaks, because the constructor never finished

question: What does delete do that C's free does not?

choices.1: It checks for double-free and aborts safely
choices.2: It runs the object's destructor before deallocating the memory via operator delete (free never calls destructors)
choices.3: It nulls out the pointer after freeing
choices.4: It always returns the memory to the OS immediately

question: Why must delete[] be used (not delete) for memory from new[]?

choices.1: delete[] returns the memory to a different heap arena
choices.2: delete[] disables exceptions during destruction
choices.3: delete[] runs the destructor for every element using the array's hidden element-count metadata; using plain delete on a new[] block is UB
choices.4: delete[] is merely a stylistic alias for delete

question: Why does a multithreaded ptmalloc use multiple arenas (often one per thread)?

choices.1: To reduce lock contention, since each arena has its own lock
choices.2: To keep each thread's memory in a different NUMA node automatically
choices.3: To give each thread a separate address space
choices.4: To allow each thread a different allocation size limit

question: Why does free() often not reduce the process's memory as seen in top/htop?

choices.1: Because free usually returns chunks to the allocator's internal bins/caches rather than to the OS; memory only truly returns for mmap'd blocks (munmap) or when a coalesced block at the top of the heap lets it shrink with brk (rare)
choices.2: Because the kernel lazily reclaims freed pages after a fixed delay
choices.3: Because top/htop cannot measure heap memory
choices.4: Because free zeroes the memory but keeps the mapping for security

question: What does placement new do?

choices.1: Constructs an object in already-allocated storage, performing no allocation itself
choices.2: Allocates memory at a kernel-chosen placement and constructs an object
choices.3: Relocates an existing object to a new address
choices.4: Allocates memory but skips construction

question: When realloc cannot grow a block in place, what is the slow path, and how does this relate to std::vector?

choices.1: It always uses mremap, so it is O(1)
choices.2: It mallocs a new block, memcpy's min(old, new) bytes, frees the old block (O(n)) — the cost of moving on growth is why vector grows by doubling to amortise it
choices.3: It zeroes the extra space and returns the same pointer
choices.4: It throws std::bad_alloc and leaves the old block untouched

question: What is the key difference between memcpy and memmove?

choices.1: memcpy runs constructors; memmove does not
choices.2: memcpy is overlap-safe; memmove requires non-overlapping regions
choices.3: memcpy requires the source and destination regions not to overlap (overlap is UB); memmove behaves correctly even when they overlap
choices.4: memcpy copies element counts; memmove copies bytes

question: Why is using the mem* functions on a non-trivially-copyable type undefined behaviour?

choices.1: Because they move raw bytes without running constructors/destructors or respecting the object model — e.g. a memcpy'd std::string aliases the same heap buffer, leading to a double free
choices.2: Because mem* cannot handle types larger than a cache line
choices.3: Because the compiler forbids passing class types to void* parameters
choices.4: Because mem* functions are slower than the type's copy constructor

question: What does memset(arr, 1, n) do to an array of ints, and why?

choices.1: Is undefined behaviour for any non-char array
choices.2: Sets each int to 1, because memset is value-aware
choices.3: Sets each int to 0x01010101, because memset fills identical bytes — only 0 and 0xFF are meaningful for multi-byte types
choices.4: Sets only the first int to 1 and leaves the rest unchanged

question: Why can a plain memset(buf, 0, n) fail to securely erase secrets?

choices.1: Because the compiler may legally eliminate the memset as a dead store if buf is never read again; use explicit_bzero, memset_s, or a volatile-pointer loop instead
choices.2: Because memset cannot write the value 0
choices.3: Because memset only writes the first byte of buf
choices.4: Because the OS re-fills freed memory with the old contents

question: Why is memcmp == 0 not a reliable test of logical equality?

choices.1: Because memcmp always returns 0 for equal-length buffers
choices.2: Because byte-identical is not logically equal: struct padding bytes are indeterminate, floats have +0.0/-0.0 and NaN quirks, and multi-byte integer ordering reflects byte order (endianness), not numeric value
choices.3: Because memcmp compares as signed char, inverting high bytes
choices.4: Because memcmp ignores the length argument

question: The mem* functions take a length argument measured in what units?

choices.1: Machine words (8 bytes each)
choices.2: Element counts, like the STL algorithms
choices.3: Bytes — for arrays you must multiply by sizeof(T), e.g. memcpy(d, s, n * sizeof(T))
choices.4: Bits

question: Passing a null pointer to memcpy when n == 0 is:

choices.1: Undefined behaviour regardless of length — a null pointer is invalid even when n == 0
choices.2: Well-defined only if the destination is also null
choices.3: Implementation-defined but never UB
choices.4: Always well-defined, since no bytes are touched

question: How does memchr differ from strchr, making it safe on binary data?

choices.1: memchr returns an index rather than a pointer
choices.2: memchr respects the explicit length n and does not stop at a NUL terminator, so it works on arbitrary binary bytes
choices.3: memchr is NUL-terminated like strchr but faster
choices.4: memchr searches backwards from the end of the buffer

question: Which C++ typed equivalents does the note recommend in place of raw mem* calls?

choices.1: std::memcpy is always preferred; there are no typed equivalents
choices.2: std::move and std::forward for all copies
choices.3: std::copy / copy_n (lower to memmove for trivially copyable types), std::fill (lowers to memset), and std::bit_cast for type punning instead of reinterpret_cast
