question: What does the note say about memcpy and object creation in C++20+?

choices.1: memcpy always requires std::launder before the bytes can be read back
choices.2: memcpy can construct objects of any type, including those with constructors
choices.3: memcpy into suitably sized/aligned storage implicitly creates objects of implicit-lifetime type there (readable without std::launder), but still does not create non-trivial objects
choices.4: memcpy now runs the copy constructor for class types automatically

question: What is the central idea of RAII?

choices.1: Tie a resource's lifetime to an object's lifetime: acquire it in the constructor and release it in the destructor, so cleanup runs on every exit path
choices.2: Use reference counting on every resource so it is freed when the last user drops it
choices.3: Defer all cleanup to a manual goto cleanup label at the end of each function
choices.4: Allocate all resources at program start and release them all at program end

question: When an exception is thrown, what guarantees RAII cleanup happens but manual cleanup may not?

choices.1: Exceptions trigger garbage collection, which reclaims both RAII objects and manually allocated resources
choices.2: Manual cleanup runs first via atexit handlers, then RAII destructors run
choices.3: The runtime scans the heap and frees any unreferenced memory before propagating the exception
choices.4: The stack unwinds and every fully-constructed local's destructor runs, whereas manual cleanup code placed after the throw point is simply skipped

question: When does an object's lifetime begin and end?

choices.1: It begins when its constructor completes (and storage is obtained) and ends when its destructor starts; accessing the object outside that window is UB
choices.2: It begins when the object is first read and ends when it is last read
choices.3: It begins at the opening brace of its scope and ends at the closing brace
choices.4: It begins when storage is allocated and ends when storage is freed, regardless of constructor/destructor

question: How do placement new and an explicit destructor call relate storage duration and lifetime?

choices.1: They decouple the two: placement new begins an object's lifetime in pre-existing storage, and an explicit ~T() ends the lifetime while the storage still exists
choices.2: Placement new ends a lifetime and the explicit destructor call begins a new one
choices.3: They merge the two so that lifetime and storage duration always coincide
choices.4: They are illegal outside of constexpr contexts

question: Why does const char* p = make().c_str(); leave p dangling (where make returns std::string by value)?

choices.1: The compiler copies the string into p, but the copy is const and immediately destroyed
choices.2: make() returns a reference to a local, which is the actual bug
choices.3: The temporary string dies at the end of the full expression (the semicolon), so p points into freed memory; lifetime extension does not chain through the c_str() call
choices.4: c_str() always returns a pointer to a static empty buffer

question: Which binding extends a temporary's lifetime beyond the end of the full expression?

choices.1: Assigning it to an auto variable by value
choices.2: Binding it to a const& or an rvalue reference (and range-for over the temporary extends it across the loop)
choices.3: Passing it by value to a function
choices.4: Taking its address with &

question: In what order are a class's non-static data members constructed and destroyed?

choices.1: Constructed in declaration order and destroyed in the same (declaration) order
choices.2: Constructed alphabetically by name, destroyed in reverse
choices.3: Constructed in declaration order (not member-initializer-list order) and destroyed in the reverse of that order
choices.4: Constructed in the order they appear in the constructor's initializer list, destroyed in the same order

question: Why does a polymorphic base class need a virtual destructor?

choices.1: Without it, deleting a derived object through a base pointer is UB because the derived destructor never runs
choices.2: Without it, the base constructor would run after the derived constructor
choices.3: Because non-virtual destructors cannot be called during stack unwinding

question: Which standard-library types are RAII owners that release in their destructors?

choices.1: None — the standard library requires manual cleanup
choices.2: Only the smart pointers unique_ptr and shared_ptr
choices.3: Only container types such as vector and string
choices.4: unique_ptr, shared_ptr, lock_guard/scoped_lock, vector, string, fstream

question: What does std::pmr (polymorphic memory resources) let you do?

choices.1: Make every allocation thread-safe and lock-free by default
choices.2: Choose a container's memory allocation strategy at runtime while keeping the container's type unchanged
choices.3: Replace new/delete globally for the whole program
choices.4: Allocate memory that is automatically garbage collected

question: What is the central abstract interface in PMR?

choices.1: std::allocator_traits, specialised for pmr
choices.2: std::pmr::memory_resource, an abstract base class with virtual do_allocate/do_deallocate/do_is_equal
choices.3: std::pmr::polymorphic_allocator, a concrete final class
choices.4: std::pmr::vector, which all resources derive from

question: What is the call chain when a std::pmr::vector allocates?

choices.1: pmr::vector -> operator new -> memory_resource -> the kernel
choices.2: pmr::vector -> polymorphic_allocator<T> -> memory_resource -> the actual allocation strategy
choices.3: pmr::vector -> memory_resource -> polymorphic_allocator<T> -> the heap
choices.4: pmr::vector -> allocator_traits -> std::allocator -> malloc

question: What does a std::pmr::polymorphic_allocator<T> actually store, and why is it 'polymorphic'?

choices.1: It stores a vtable of allocation functions generated per type T
choices.2: It stores a pointer to a memory_resource; it is polymorphic because the exact resource (and thus strategy) can vary at runtime through that pointer
choices.3: It stores a template parameter selecting the strategy at compile time
choices.4: It stores an inline copy of the buffer it allocates from, fixed at compile time

question: Why can two std::pmr::vector<int> objects share one type even with different allocation strategies, unlike std::vector<int, MyAllocator<int>>?

choices.2: Because polymorphic_allocator is type-erased into void* at every call
choices.3: Because std::vector<int, MyAllocator<int>> is actually the same type as std::vector<int>
choices.4: Because pmr containers ignore their allocator entirely

question: What is the behaviour of std::pmr::monotonic_buffer_resource?

choices.1: It always forwards directly to global new/delete
choices.2: It maintains size-class pools and frees each object individually
choices.3: It reference-counts allocations and frees them when the count hits zero
choices.4: It allocates from a buffer and typically releases everything at once — ideal for short-lived groups of objects

question: How do unsynchronized_pool_resource and synchronized_pool_resource differ?

choices.1: They are identical aliases kept for backward compatibility
choices.2: unsynchronized is for single allocations; synchronized is for arrays
choices.3: unsynchronized uses the heap; synchronized uses a stack buffer
choices.4: unsynchronized uses pools without thread synchronization (faster, not thread-safe); synchronized adds synchronization so it is safe across threads

question: What does std::pmr::new_delete_resource() use under the hood?

choices.1: A thread-local pool per calling thread
choices.2: A stack-allocated monotonic buffer
choices.3: The normal global new and delete
choices.4: mmap directly, bypassing the C++ allocator

question: In the example using a std::array<std::byte,1024> with a monotonic_buffer_resource, where does the pmr::vector get its memory?

choices.1: From the local stack buffer first, falling back to the heap only if the buffer is exhausted
choices.2: From a global memory pool shared by all pmr containers
choices.3: From thread-local storage allocated at thread start
choices.4: Always directly from the heap, ignoring the buffer

question: What does std::launder(p) do?

choices.1: It starts the lifetime of a new T object at that address
choices.2: It reinterprets the bytes at p as a T, enabling type punning
choices.3: It fixes a misaligned pointer so the object can be accessed safely
choices.4: It returns a usable pointer to a real, already-live object of type T at the same address, acting as an optimization/provenance barrier

question: Where is std::launder declared and what is its signature?

choices.1: In <type_traits>: template<class T> constexpr T launder(T p)
choices.2: In <memory>: template<class T> T* launder(void* p)
choices.3: In <new>: template<class T> constexpr T* launder(T* p) noexcept
choices.4: In <cstring>: void* launder(void* p, size_t n)

question: Which of these does std::launder NOT do?

choices.1: Return a pointer the abstract machine may use to refer to the current object at that address
choices.2: Help after certain placement-new reuse cases where old pointers are stale
choices.3: Create an object, start a lifetime, fix bad alignment, change the bytes, or make type punning legal
choices.4: Act as a provenance/optimization barrier

question: Under the transparent replacement rule, when do you NOT need std::launder after destroying and reconstructing an object in the same storage?

choices.2: Only when the storage is dynamically allocated
choices.3: Only when the old object was const
choices.4: When it is the same storage, the same type (ignoring top-level cv), the old object was not const, and it is a complete object (or matching subobject) — the old name/pointer retargets automatically

question: Which situation requires std::launder because transparent replacement fails?

choices.1: Any time you call placement new, without exception
choices.2: The old object was const, you are dealing with base-class subobjects, or you only hold a pointer derived from raw byte storage / an object of a different dynamic type now lives there
choices.3: Whenever the object is larger than a cache line
choices.4: Only when allocating with malloc instead of new

question: After new (const_cast<X*>(p)) const X{5}; over a previously const X, why is reading p->n directly UB while std::launder(p)->n is OK?

choices.1: Because the old object was const, the old pointer p does not automatically refer to the new object; launder yields a valid pointer to the newly constructed one
choices.2: Because launder physically copies the new value to where p points
choices.3: Because p->n reads uninitialized memory until launder zero-initializes it

question: What is the important caveat about placement-new'ing into the storage of a const complete object?

choices.1: It is fine for static objects but UB for automatic ones
choices.2: It is always fine as long as you launder the result afterward

question: Why can std::launder NOT be used to make *p valid over raw uninitialized storage?

choices.1: Because launder zeroes the storage, destroying any value you wrote
choices.2: Because launder requires the storage to be heap-allocated
choices.3: Because launder does not begin an object's lifetime; you must first create the object (e.g. placement new or std::construct_at) — launder only yields a pointer to an object that is already alive

question: Why is std::launder not a type-punning tool (e.g. launder an int* over a float)?

choices.1: Because launder<int> silently converts the float's value to an int
choices.2: Because launder only changes alignment, not type
choices.3: Because punning is fine; the example is actually well-defined
choices.4: Because launder<int> requires an actual live int object at that address; if a float lives there, no int exists — use bit_cast/memcpy/start_lifetime_as instead

question: In the base/derived placement-new example, what role does cppreference assign to std::launder?

question: What are the four storage durations in C++?

choices.1: Block, file, process, and kernel
choices.2: Stack, heap, register, and constant
choices.3: Automatic, static, thread, and dynamic
choices.4: Local, global, shared, and mapped

question: What characterises automatic storage duration?

choices.1: Objects are created when execution enters their block and destroyed when it leaves — this covers most local variables
choices.2: Objects are created and destroyed manually with new/delete
choices.3: Objects live for the entire program
choices.4: Objects get one instance per thread

question: Which of these has static storage duration?

choices.1: Only function parameters
choices.2: Only variables declared thread_local
choices.3: Global variables, namespace-scope variables, static local variables, and static data members
choices.4: Only variables declared with the new keyword

question: What is true of an object with thread storage duration (thread_local)?

choices.1: It lives for the entire program regardless of threads
choices.2: All threads share a single instance protected by an implicit mutex
choices.3: Each thread gets its own separate instance, created when the thread begins or first accesses it and destroyed when the thread ends
choices.4: It is destroyed at the end of the enclosing block

question: How is dynamic storage duration controlled?

choices.1: By the linker at program startup
choices.2: Manually via new/delete, or indirectly via smart pointers and containers (allocator-controlled)
choices.3: Automatically when the enclosing block exits
choices.4: Per-thread by the runtime's TLS machinery

question: What is the distinction between storage duration and scope?

choices.1: Storage duration applies to functions; scope applies to variables
choices.2: Storage duration describes where the name is visible; scope describes how long memory lives
choices.3: Storage duration describes how long the storage exists; scope describes where the name can be used
choices.4: They are two names for the same concept

question: A static int count = 0; inside a function has which storage duration, and what is the effect?

choices.1: Automatic storage duration — it is recreated each call
choices.2: Static storage duration — it is allocated once and retains its value between calls
choices.3: Thread storage duration — each thread gets its own count
choices.4: Dynamic storage duration — it must be deleted manually

question: What does the auto keyword mean in modern C++ (C++11 and later)?

choices.1: It still requests automatic (stack) storage duration
choices.2: It is a type-deduction keyword and has nothing to do with storage duration anymore
choices.3: It gives the variable static storage duration
choices.4: It allocates the variable in a CPU register

question: What does static do at namespace/global scope?

choices.1: Makes it thread_local automatically
choices.2: Allocates it on the stack of main()
choices.3: Gives the variable internal linkage, so it is visible only within that translation unit
choices.4: Gives the variable external linkage so other files can see it

question: Inside a class, what does declaring a data member static do?

choices.1: Forces the member to be initialized in every constructor
choices.2: Gives each instance its own private copy
choices.3: Makes the member mutable inside const methods
choices.4: Makes it shared among all instances — one copy existing for the program's lifetime

question: What does extern do?

choices.1: Defines a new global variable with internal linkage
choices.2: Marks a variable as thread-local across all modules
choices.3: Declares a variable or function that is defined elsewhere (another TU), giving external linkage; extern "C" also requests C linkage to avoid name mangling
choices.4: Allocates a variable dynamically on the heap

question: What does mutable do, and what does it not affect?

choices.1: It gives a member external linkage
choices.2: It makes a member thread-local
choices.3: It extends a member's lifetime to the whole program
choices.4: It allows a data member to be modified even inside a const object; it does not affect storage duration

question: What does thread_local specify?

choices.1: A variable stored in a CPU register
choices.2: A variable visible only within one function call
choices.3: Each thread gets its own instance, living from thread start to thread exit
choices.4: A single shared instance guarded by a mutex

question: What is the status of the register specifier?

choices.1: It is the default for all local variables
choices.2: It gives a variable static storage duration
choices.3: It forces the variable into a register and is mandatory for hot loops
choices.4: It was a hint to store a local in a CPU register and is deprecated as of C++17

choices.3: Each access issues a system call to the kernel's TLS manager

choices.2: Because the compiler marks them volatile, forcing fresh reads

choices.4: Because they are always stored in registers, never memory

question: What does declaring a namespace inline do to its members?

choices.1: It forces all functions in it to be inlined by the compiler
choices.2: It prevents the members from being referenced by their fully-qualified name
choices.3: It merges it permanently with the global namespace
choices.4: It makes them visible in the enclosing namespace as if declared there, so (with inline v2) mylib::Widget refers to mylib::v2::Widget

question: What is the primary use case for inline namespaces?

choices.1: API/ABI versioning: expose a current default version while keeping older versions explicitly available
choices.2: Reducing compile times by inlining template instantiations
choices.3: Allowing two functions with the same signature to coexist as overloads
choices.4: Hiding implementation symbols from the linker entirely

question: Given inline namespace v2, are mylib::v1::Widget and mylib::v2::Widget the same type?

choices.1: Yes — only if they have identical members
choices.2: Yes — inline makes them aliases of one another
choices.3: No — mylib::v1::Widget is not a real type at all
choices.4: No — they are distinct types, even though mylib::v2::Widget can also be named mylib::Widget

question: What is the ABI-relevant effect of inline namespaces?

choices.1: They are part of mangled names, so v1 and v2 generally produce different linker symbols, letting old and new APIs coexist in one library
choices.2: They have no effect on mangled names, only on source-level lookup
choices.3: They strip version information from symbols to keep the ABI stable
choices.4: They force C linkage on all enclosed symbols

question: Why are inline namespaces NOT a complete ABI-management system?

choices.1: They handle C++ name/symbol separation but do not replace shared-library versioning (.so.1 vs .so.2), ELF symbol versioning, exported-symbol control, or binary-compatibility rules
choices.2: They require every minor release to bump the namespace, which is impractical
choices.3: They only work for functions, not types, so half the ABI is unmanaged
choices.4: They break name mangling, so the linker cannot resolve any symbol

question: What is the risk of inline-namespace versioning for a library that exposes many templates?

choices.1: The compiler instantiates each template twice, doubling binary size automatically
choices.2: Inline namespaces disable template argument deduction
choices.3: Instantiations like std::vector<mylib::v1::Widget> and std::vector<mylib::v2::Widget> are entirely different types, making interop between versions awkward
choices.4: Templates cannot be placed inside an inline namespace at all

question: What does switching which namespace is inline (e.g. from v1 to v2) change when users recompile?

choices.1: Nothing — the inline keyword is purely documentation
choices.2: It changes what the unqualified name (e.g. mylib::Widget) resolves to — usually intended, but can be surprising
choices.3: It forces all dependents to be rewritten to use fully-qualified names
choices.4: It deletes the old version's symbols from the library

question: What is the note's practical recommendation for using inline namespaces?

choices.1: Avoid them entirely in favour of preprocessor macros
choices.2: Use them for major ABI-breaking versions (e.g. abi_v1/abi_v2/abi_v3), not for every minor release
choices.3: Use them only for internal, non-exported code
choices.4: Use a new inline namespace for every patch release (v1_0_1, v1_0_2, ...)

question: What linkage do declarations inside an unnamed namespace have?

choices.2: External linkage — other translation units can refer to them by name
choices.3: No linkage at all — they cannot even be used within the same file

choices.2: Nothing — static is strictly more powerful
choices.3: Only macros and preprocessor symbols
choices.4: Only global variables, never functions

question: Conceptually, what does the compiler do for an unnamed namespace?

choices.1: It marks the members inline so they are merged across translation units
choices.2: It invents a unique hidden namespace name per translation unit and applies a using-directive, so each .cpp gets its own distinct entities

question: If a.cpp and b.cpp each contain namespace { int counter = 0; }, what is true?

question: Why should you avoid putting an unnamed namespace in a header?

question: What is the recommended practical rule for unnamed namespaces vs static?

choices.3: Use an unnamed namespace for private implementation details in a .cpp file; reserve namespace-scope static for the older C-style single variable/function case

question: Why can't you achieve the same hiding for a helper struct with namespace-scope static?

choices.2: Because a static struct is implicitly a template
choices.3: Because static applies to objects and functions, not to type declarations
choices.4: Because static types are only allowed inside functions

question: Into which three fields does IEEE 754 split a floating-point number?

choices.1: Mantissa, base, and precision flag
choices.2: Sign bit, (biased) exponent, and fraction/mantissa, giving (-1)^s x mantissa x 2^exponent
choices.3: Integer part, fractional part, and rounding mode
choices.4: Sign bit, magnitude, and checksum

question: What is the bit layout of a 32-bit single-precision float?

choices.1: 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, 23 fraction bits
choices.2: 1 sign bit, 7 exponent bits, 24 fraction bits
choices.3: 1 sign bit, 11 exponent bits, 52 fraction bits
choices.4: 1 sign bit, 10 exponent bits, 21 fraction bits

question: What is the bit layout of a 64-bit double-precision float?

choices.1: 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, 55 fraction bits
choices.2: 1 sign bit, 11 exponent bits, 52 fraction bits
choices.3: 1 sign bit, 16 exponent bits, 47 fraction bits
choices.4: 1 sign bit, 12 exponent bits, 51 fraction bits

question: Why does IEEE 754 store a biased exponent rather than the exponent directly?

choices.1: So that both positive and negative exponents fit in unsigned exponent bits without a separate sign; actual = stored - bias (127 for float, 1023 for double)
choices.2: So that the exponent can be larger than the fraction field
choices.3: Because biasing makes multiplication of two floats exact

question: What is the 'hidden bit' (implicit leading 1) for normal numbers?

choices.2: An extra sign bit stored separately from the main sign
choices.3: Normal numbers are interpreted as 1.fraction, so the leading 1 is assumed and not stored, saving one bit of precision
choices.4: The most significant bit of the exponent, reused as a flag

question: What is the value formula for a normal 32-bit float?

choices.1: (-1)^s x 1.fraction x 2^(E - 127)
choices.2: (-1)^s x fraction x 10^(E - 127)
choices.3: (-1)^s x 1.fraction x 2^(E - 1023)
choices.4: (-1)^s x 0.fraction x 2^(E - 127)

question: How is infinity encoded in IEEE 754?

choices.2: Exponent all 1s and fraction not all 0s
choices.3: Exponent all 0s and fraction all 0s
choices.4: Exponent all 1s and fraction all 0s (sign bit picks +inf or -inf)

question: How is NaN distinguished from infinity, given both use an all-1s exponent?

choices.2: NaN has an all-zero fraction, whereas infinity has a non-zero fraction
choices.3: NaN has a non-zero fraction, whereas infinity has an all-zero fraction
choices.4: They are indistinguishable; only the operation that produced them tells you

question: How are +0 and -0 represented, and how can they behave differently?

choices.1: They have identical bit patterns and can never behave differently
choices.2: -0 is represented as the smallest subnormal rather than a true zero
choices.3: Exponent all 1s and fraction all 0s, distinguished by sign
choices.4: Exponent and fraction all 0s, with the sign bit giving +0 vs -0; e.g. 1/+0 = +inf but 1/-0 = -inf, preserving directional information on underflow

question: How is a subnormal number encoded and interpreted for a 32-bit float?

choices.2: Any exponent with the hidden bit set to 0, interpreted as 1.fraction x 2^(E-127)
choices.3: Exponent all 1s with a non-zero fraction, interpreted as 1.fraction x 2^127
choices.4: Exponent all 0s with a non-zero fraction, interpreted as 0.fraction x 2^-126 (no implicit leading 1), enabling gradual underflow

question: What is the difference between a quiet NaN and a signaling NaN?

choices.2: A quiet NaN equals itself under ==, while a signaling NaN does not
choices.3: A quiet NaN propagates through calculations without immediately raising an exception; a signaling NaN is intended to signal an invalid operation when used

question: Why is 0.1 not represented exactly, and what is the default rounding mode?

choices.2: 0.1 is exact, but the default round-down mode corrupts it

choices.4: 0.1 overflows the exponent field; the default mode is round toward zero

question: What is the value range of an n-bit unsigned integer?

choices.2: 1 to 2^n

question: What is the value range of an n-bit two's complement signed integer?

choices.3: -2^n to 2^n

question: How do you form -x in two's complement?

choices.2: Flip all the bits of x (one's complement only)
choices.3: Set the most significant bit of x to 1
choices.4: Flip all the bits of x, then add 1

question: Why does two's complement let the same addition hardware work for signed and unsigned values?

choices.1: Because arithmetic wraps modulo 2^n: -5 has the same bit pattern as unsigned 251 (256-5), so ordinary binary addition produces the correct result with the carry-out discarded
choices.2: Because negative numbers are stored in a hidden second register
choices.3: Because two's complement stores the sign separately from the magnitude
choices.4: Because the hardware inspects the sign bit and switches to a separate subtraction unit

question: In two's complement, what is special about the leftmost (most significant) bit's place value?

choices.1: It has the same positive place value as in unsigned, plus a sign flag elsewhere
choices.2: It stores the rounding direction
choices.3: It has a negative place value (e.g. -2^(n-1) = -128 for 8 bits), so a value can be read directly by summing place values
choices.4: It is always ignored and used only as a parity check

question: Which is an advantage of two's complement over sign-magnitude or one's complement?

choices.1: It can represent a strictly larger positive range than unsigned of the same width
choices.2: There is only one representation of zero, addition/subtraction work naturally, the sign bit shows negativity, and hardware is simpler
choices.3: It has two distinct representations of zero, aiding error detection
choices.4: It stores the sign in a separate dedicated byte

question: In 4-bit two's complement, what decimal value does the pattern 1000 represent?
What form does a normalized floating-point number take?

choices.1: 1.fraction x 2^e, with the leading 1 implicit (the hidden bit), so it isn't stored
choices.2: 1.fraction x 2^e, with the leading 1 stored explicitly in the fraction field
choices.3: 0.fraction x 2^e, with no leading bit stored
choices.4: fraction x 10^e, in normalized decimal

question: How does a subnormal number differ in form from a normalized one?

choices.1: It has the form 1.fraction x 2^emax — it gains a second hidden bit
choices.2: It stores the exponent in two's complement instead of biased form
choices.3: It uses base 10 instead of base 2
choices.4: It has the form 0.fraction x 2^emin — it drops the implicit leading 1

question: Why do subnormal numbers exist?

choices.1: To store NaN payloads compactly
choices.2: To fill the gap between the smallest normalized value and zero, providing gradual underflow instead of jumping straight to 0
choices.3: To represent numbers larger than the maximum normalized value
choices.4: To speed up multiplication of very small numbers

question: What is the precision tradeoff of subnormal numbers?

choices.1: They have more precision because they use the whole fraction field for magnitude
choices.2: They have infinite precision but a restricted exponent range
choices.3: They have less precision than normalized numbers: lacking the implicit leading 1, they retain fewer significant bits as values approach zero
choices.4: They have identical precision to normalized numbers

question: What does std::numeric_limits<T>::min() return for a floating-point type T?

choices.1: The smallest positive subnormal value
choices.2: The most negative finite value
choices.3: The smallest positive normalized value
choices.4: Negative infinity

question: What does std::numeric_limits<T>::denorm_min() return for a floating-point type T?

choices.1: The smallest positive subnormal value
choices.2: The most negative finite value
choices.3: The smallest positive normalized value
choices.4: Zero

question: Which ordering correctly relates these floating-point quantities?

choices.1: denorm_min() < 0 < min() < max()
choices.2: 0 < denorm_min() < min() < max()
choices.3: 0 < max() < min() < denorm_min()
choices.4: 0 < min() < denorm_min() < max()

question: What does std::numeric_limits<T>::lowest() return for a floating-point type T?

choices.1: The most negative finite value
choices.2: The smallest positive subnormal value
choices.3: The smallest positive normalized value
choices.4: Negative infinity

question: How does converting a floating-point value to an integer (static_cast<int>) behave?

choices.1: It always rounds down (floor), even for negatives
choices.2: It truncates toward zero (3.9 -> 3, -3.9 -> -3); it does not round to nearest
choices.3: It rounds to nearest, ties to even
choices.4: It rounds to the nearest integer, ties away from zero

question: Why does double x = a / b; (with int a=5, b=2) give 2.0 rather than 2.5?

choices.1: / on ints is undefined, so the compiler substitutes 2.0
choices.2: The assignment rounds 2.5 down to 2.0
choices.3: a / b is integer division, evaluated first to 2, then converted to double; cast one operand (static_cast<double>(a)/b) to fix it
choices.4: double cannot store 2.5, so it truncates to 2.0

question: On a 32-bit unsigned int, what does static_cast<unsigned int>(-1) produce, and why?

choices.1: It is undefined behaviour
choices.2: 0, because negatives clamp to the minimum unsigned value
choices.3: -1, because the bit pattern is preserved and reinterpreted as signed
choices.4: 4294967295 (2^32 - 1), because signed-to-unsigned conversion is modulo 2^32

question: Why does (x < y) with int x=-1 and unsigned int y=1 often evaluate to false?

choices.1: Comparing signed and unsigned is undefined, so the result is arbitrary
choices.2: y is converted to signed -1, making it x < -1
choices.3: x is converted to unsigned before the comparison, becoming a huge value (~4294967295), so the comparison is effectively 4294967295 < 1
choices.4: The compiler treats -1 as 0 in unsigned context

question: Up to what magnitude can a double represent consecutive integers exactly, and what's the practical rule?

choices.1: Up to 2^53 (53 bits of precision); beyond that adjacent integers are indistinguishable, so don't store order IDs, timestamps, or large counters in a double
choices.2: Up to 2^31, after which it overflows to infinity
choices.3: Up to 2^23, matching the float fraction width
choices.4: Up to 2^64, since double is 64 bits wide

question: What is the effect of brace initialization on a narrowing conversion, e.g. int x{3.9};?

choices.1: It is undefined behaviour at runtime
choices.2: It is a compile-time error (narrowing), whereas int x = 3.9; compiles and silently becomes 3
choices.3: It compiles and truncates 3.9 to 3, identical to copy-initialization
choices.4: It compiles and rounds 3.9 to 4

question: What does static_cast<unsigned char>(300) yield on a system with 8-bit unsigned char?

choices.1: It is a compile-time error
choices.2: 0, because 300 overflows to wrap to zero
choices.3: 44, because unsigned conversion is modulo 256 (300 mod 256)
choices.4: 255, because the value is clamped to the maximum

question: Why is static_cast<int>(x) dangerous when x may be NaN or infinity (or 1e100)?

choices.1: It silently yields 0, which is usually acceptable
choices.2: Converting a non-finite or out-of-range floating value to int is unreliable/UB; guard with std::isfinite and an explicit range check first
choices.3: It always yields INT_MAX, which is safe to rely on
choices.4: It rounds NaN to the nearest representable int

question: Which cast does the note recommend for ordinary numeric conversions, over a C-style cast?

choices.1: reinterpret_cast, to preserve the bit pattern
choices.2: static_cast, e.g. static_cast<double>(i) — more idiomatic and explicit than (double)i
choices.3: dynamic_cast, for safe runtime-checked conversion
choices.4: const_cast, to strip qualifiers before converting

question: What is the rule of thumb for choosing member vs free (non-member) operators?

choices.1: Members for comparison operators; free functions for assignment operators
choices.2: Free functions for everything, since members cannot access private data
choices.3: Members for all binary operators; free functions only for unary operators
choices.4: Members for operators that modify the left operand or are object-intrinsic (=, +=, [], (), ->, conversions); free functions for symmetric binary operators (+, ==, <, <<)

question: Why should symmetric binary operators like operator+ be free functions?

choices.1: Because the standard forbids operator+ from being a member
choices.2: Because free functions are always faster than member functions
choices.3: Because member operators cannot return by value
choices.4: So implicit conversion works on both operands; as a member, 2 + money would fail because the left side must already be a Money to find the member, while money + 2 would work

question: What is the canonical pattern for + and += to avoid duplicated logic?

choices.1: Define + as a member and derive += from it inside the class
choices.2: Define compound assignment (+=) as a member, then define binary + as a free function implemented in terms of +=
choices.3: Define + only and let the compiler synthesise += automatically
choices.4: Define both independently with the same body copied into each

question: Why must operator<< for stream output be a free function?

choices.1: Because members cannot return references
choices.2: Because std::ostream is final and forbids member operators
choices.3: Because its left operand is std::ostream, not your type, so it cannot be a member of your type
choices.4: Because << must be constexpr, which members cannot be

question: What is a 'hidden friend' in this context, and what is its benefit?

choices.1: A friend function defined inside the class body — a free function with private access that is found only by argument-dependent lookup, keeping overload sets clean
choices.2: A virtual operator hidden behind the vtable
choices.3: A private member operator that only the class itself can call
choices.4: A macro-generated operator that bypasses access control

question: Why provide both const and non-const overloads of operator[] (or operator())?

choices.1: So the type works through both const and mutable references — returning a mutable reference for non-const access and a const reference/value for const access
choices.2: Because otherwise the operator cannot be called at all
choices.3: Because operator[] must always be overloaded an even number of times
choices.4: Because the const version handles indexing and the non-const version handles assignment exclusively

question: What is the guidance on conversion operators?

choices.1: Conversion operators must be free functions, never members
choices.2: Keep them explicit unless you truly want implicit conversion, since implicit conversions are a classic source of surprising overload resolution
choices.3: Make them virtual so derived types can override the conversion
choices.4: Always make them implicit so users don't need static_cast

question: How are prefix and postfix increment distinguished, and which is cheaper?

choices.1: Postfix takes a dummy int parameter and must return the old value by copy; prefix returns *this by reference and is cheaper, so prefer ++it when the result is unused
choices.2: Prefix takes a dummy int parameter; postfix returns by reference and is cheaper
choices.3: They are distinguished by return type alone; both cost the same
choices.4: Postfix is always optimised to prefix by the compiler, so it makes no difference

question: Which statement about operator-overloading rules is correct?

choices.1: You may redefine int + int as long as the result type differs
choices.2: You can change an operator's precedence by annotating it
choices.3: You cannot invent new operators or change arity/precedence, ., ::, ?:, and sizeof cannot be overloaded, and at least one operand must be a user-defined type
choices.4: Every operator including :: and ?: can be overloaded for user types

question: What does a <=> b return?

choices.1: An int: -1, 0, or +1
choices.2: An enum that is always std::strong_ordering
choices.3: A bool that is true when a < b
choices.4: A comparison category object (strong/weak/partial_ordering) that you compare against 0 — not a bool

question: What does `auto operator<=>(const T&) const = default;` generate?

choices.1: All six operators including == and !=, with no need to default anything else
choices.2: Lexicographic, member-by-member comparison yielding <, <=, >, and >=
choices.3: A hash function for use in unordered containers
choices.4: Only operator< ; the others must still be written by hand

question: Why is operator== kept separate from a defaulted operator<=>?

choices.1: Because == cannot be defaulted at all and must always be written manually
choices.2: Because == returns a comparison category while <=> returns a bool
choices.3: Because <=> only works on arithmetic types, while == works on class types
choices.4: Equality can often be computed faster than full ordering (e.g. comparing string length first), so the standard lets you optimize it independently; default both

question: Why does double yield std::partial_ordering rather than strong_ordering?

choices.1: Because double comparisons are evaluated at compile time
choices.2: Because floating-point equality is undefined behaviour
choices.3: Because double has lower precision than int
choices.4: Because NaN <=> x is unordered — neither <, ==, nor > holds — so the order is only partial

question: Which comparison category fits a type where equivalent values are not necessarily identical, e.g. a case-insensitive string?

choices.1: std::weak_ordering
choices.2: std::strong_ordering
choices.3: std::partial_ordering
choices.4: std::equal_ordering

question: How does the compiler use <=> to evaluate a < b?

choices.1: It rewrites a < b as (a <=> b) < 0, and also synthesizes reversed forms so mixed-type comparisons work both ways
choices.2: It calls a dedicated operator< that <=> secretly defines as a member
choices.3: It evaluates a <=> b and a == b and ANDs the results
choices.4: It converts both operands to int and subtracts them

question: When members should not simply compare in declaration order, what is the idiom for a custom operator<=>?

choices.1: Throw if the members differ and return strong_ordering::equal otherwise
choices.2: Default it and rely on the compiler reordering members by importance
choices.3: Compare fields in priority order with early return (if (auto c = x <=> o.x; c != 0) return c; ...), choosing the return category yourself
choices.4: Return the difference of the first members as an int

question: How can you obtain a total order over floating-point members (deterministically ordering NaNs)?

choices.1: Use std::partial_order, which never reports unordered
choices.2: Use std::strong_order(a, b)
choices.3: Use a <=> b directly, which orders NaN as less than everything
choices.4: Cast both to int before comparing

question: What does Pimpl stand for and structurally consist of?

choices.1: Preprocessor implementation: header-only inlined methods
choices.2: Private member list: a macro that marks all members private
choices.3: Polymorphic implementation: a base interface with virtual methods
choices.4: Pointer to implementation: a small public class in the header holding a pointer to a separate Impl class defined in the .cpp

question: What is the main purpose of Pimpl?

choices.1: Implementation hiding — keeping private data and details out of the header so users need not know about them
choices.2: Achieving runtime polymorphism without virtual functions
choices.3: Making a class trivially copyable
choices.4: Eliminating all heap allocation from a class

question: How does Pimpl reduce compile-time dependencies?

choices.1: It compiles the implementation lazily at runtime
choices.2: Headers needed only by the implementation are included in the .cpp, so the public header can stay lightweight (e.g. just <memory>), cutting rebuild times
choices.3: It forces the compiler to precompile the header once and cache it
choices.4: It removes all templates from the codebase

question: How does Pimpl help binary/ABI compatibility?

choices.1: It versions every symbol with an inline namespace automatically
choices.2: It prevents name mangling of the public methods
choices.3: It guarantees the same memory address across program runs
choices.4: The public class layout is just a single pointer, so you can add or change members of Impl without changing the public class's size or layout

question: What are the costs of Pimpl?

choices.1: It forces every method to be virtual
choices.2: Loss of const-correctness and exception safety
choices.3: An extra heap allocation, an extra pointer indirection, more boilerplate, and care needed around copy/move/destructor
choices.4: It doubles the size of every object

question: Why is Widget::~Widget() usually defined (e.g. = default) in the .cpp rather than the header?

choices.1: Because the header must avoid including <memory>
choices.2: Because Impl is an incomplete type in the header, and unique_ptr needs the complete definition of Impl to destroy it
choices.3: Because the compiler forbids inline destructors for any class with a pointer member
choices.4: Because destructors cannot be defaulted in a header

question: What does the singleton pattern ensure?

choices.1: That a class can never be instantiated
choices.2: That each thread gets its own instance of a class
choices.3: That a class has exactly one instance and provides a global access point to it
choices.4: That a class is copyable but not movable

question: Why is the Meyers singleton (a function-local static) preferred in modern C++?

choices.1: The static local is initialized on first call, and since C++11 that initialization is guaranteed thread-safe
choices.2: It is the only form that works before main() runs
choices.3: It avoids needing a private constructor
choices.4: It allows multiple instances when needed

question: Why are the copy and move constructors/assignments deleted in a singleton?

choices.1: Because deleting them is required to make the constructor private
choices.2: To make the type trivially copyable
choices.3: To prevent users from accidentally creating additional instances (e.g. Logger copy = Logger::instance();)
choices.4: Because move semantics are incompatible with static storage

question: Why is the pointer-based singleton (new Logger() with a static pointer) usually worse than the Meyers version?

choices.1: It cannot provide a global access point
choices.2: It is not automatically thread-safe, may leak memory, has harder destruction-order management, and needs more manual code
choices.3: It always constructs the instance eagerly at program start
choices.4: It prevents the class from having any member functions

question: What are common downsides of overusing singletons?

choices.1: They force every method to be virtual and slow
choices.2: Hidden global state, tight coupling, awkward unit testing, unclear lifetime/destruction order, and difficulty substituting a mock
choices.3: They require dynamic_cast at every call site
choices.4: They cannot be accessed from more than one translation unit

question: Which alternative does the note suggest to make dependencies explicit instead of hiding them behind Logger::instance()?

choices.1: Making the class a template
choices.2: Using a thread_local instance per call
choices.3: Global variables with external linkage
choices.4: Dependency injection — passing the dependency (e.g. a Logger&) into the constructor

question: What does a C-style union do, and what is its size?

choices.1: It stores only pointers to its members; its size is one pointer
choices.2: It stores a tagged list of all members; its size grows dynamically
choices.3: It stores all its members side by side, like a struct; its size is the sum of the members
choices.4: It stores one of several possible types in the same memory location; its size is at least that of its largest member

question: What is the key safety rule for a union?

choices.1: All members can be read at any time as long as they were initialized once
choices.2: The first declared member is always the valid one
choices.3: Only one member is meaningful (active) at a time; reading a member other than the one last written is not safe
choices.4: Members can be read in any order as long as they have the same size

question: What is a tagged union and why is it the safe pattern?

choices.1: A union that automatically stores a type id in its high bits
choices.2: A struct pairing an enum tag with the union; the tag records which member is active, since a raw union does not remember it
choices.3: A union wrapped in a class with virtual functions
choices.4: A union whose members are all tagged const so they cannot change

question: Why is type punning by writing one union member and reading another problematic in C++?

choices.1: It only works for members of identical size
choices.2: It is fine in C++ but undefined in C
choices.3: It always crashes at runtime due to alignment faults
choices.4: It is compiler- and standard-sensitive (UB in C++); the safer approach is std::memcpy (or std::bit_cast)

question: What does the note recommend instead of raw unions in C++?

choices.1: A polymorphic class hierarchy with dynamic_cast
choices.2: std::any, which is faster than a union
choices.3: A std::tuple holding all the alternatives
choices.4: std::variant, which tracks the active type and handles construction/destruction safely

question: Which mental model best describes a union?

choices.1: One memory box with multiple possible labels, only one label valid at a time — not a struct holding all members at once
choices.2: An array whose elements have different types
choices.3: A pointer that can point to one of several objects
choices.4: A struct that holds every member simultaneously

question: What is dynamic dispatch?

choices.1: Selecting a template specialization during instantiation
choices.2: The mechanism that decides which overridden virtual function to call at runtime, based on the actual object type
choices.3: Resolving a function pointer stored in a global table at link time
choices.4: The compiler choosing an overload at compile time from the argument types

question: Under what conditions does dynamic dispatch occur?

choices.1: Only when the object is allocated with new
choices.2: Whenever any member function is called on any object
choices.3: Only inside templates instantiated with polymorphic types
choices.4: When a virtual function is called through a base-class pointer or reference

question: What are the vtable and vptr, and when does a class get them?

choices.1: They exist only for classes that use multiple inheritance
choices.2: The vtable is a per-class table of function pointers for virtual functions; the vptr is a hidden per-object pointer to its class's vtable. A class becomes polymorphic (and gets them) when it has at least one virtual function
choices.3: The vtable is per-object and the vptr is per-class; every class has both
choices.4: The vtable stores data members and the vptr points to the heap block

question: Without virtual, what does Animal* a = new Dog(); a->speak(); call, and why?

choices.1: Animal::speak(), because non-virtual calls use static dispatch based on the declared pointer type
choices.2: Dog::speak(), because the object is really a Dog
choices.3: It is undefined behaviour
choices.4: It is a compile error without virtual

question: What is object slicing in Animal a = d; (where d is a Dog), and its effect on a.speak()?

choices.1: Only the Animal part of d is copied into a, so a is a real Animal; a.speak() calls Animal::speak()
choices.2: It is a compile error because Dog cannot convert to Animal
choices.3: a becomes a dangling reference to d
choices.4: The whole Dog is copied, so a.speak() still calls Dog::speak()

question: Why must dynamic dispatch go through a pointer or reference rather than a by-value object?

choices.1: Pointers are faster, so the compiler only bothers with dispatch for them
choices.2: References disable dispatch; only pointers enable it
choices.3: A by-value base object holds only the base subobject (it was sliced), so the call binds statically to the base function; a pointer/reference can refer to the full derived object and consult its vptr
choices.4: By-value objects do not have a vptr at all, ever

question: What is the benign, recommended use of multiple inheritance?

choices.1: Replacing composition entirely
choices.2: Inheriting the same base twice to get two copies of its data
choices.3: Inheriting from multiple interfaces — abstract classes with no state
choices.4: Inheriting deep stateful class hierarchies to reuse data members

question: How is a multiply-inheriting object laid out, and what is the consequence for pointers?

choices.1: The layout is unspecified and base pointers can never be formed
choices.2: Bases are laid out sequentially as subobjects; casting to a non-first base may change the address (the compiler adjusts on static_cast), and each polymorphic base carries its own vptr — so two polymorphic bases mean two vptrs
choices.3: All bases share offset 0, so base pointers always have the same address as the derived pointer
choices.4: Bases are stored in a separate heap block reached via one shared vptr

question: What is the diamond problem?

choices.1: When two bases share a common base, a naive layout duplicates that base, making its members and upcasts to it ambiguous
choices.2: When a class inherits from itself recursively
choices.3: When two virtual functions in different bases have the same name
choices.4: When a derived class has more vptrs than its bases

question: How does virtual inheritance (: virtual Base) fix the diamond problem?

choices.1: It deletes the duplicate base at link time
choices.2: It converts the base into an interface with no data
choices.3: It renames one of the duplicated bases automatically
choices.4: It makes the derived classes share exactly one common base subobject, so member access and upcasts become unambiguous

question: What is the cost of virtual inheritance?

choices.1: It doubles the number of data members in the object
choices.2: It forces every member access to go through dynamic_cast
choices.3: It disables all virtual function calls
choices.4: The shared base can sit at different offsets, so the object stores a vbase pointer/offset and reaching virtual-base members needs an extra indirection; the most-derived class is responsible for constructing the virtual base

question: Which standard library hierarchy is the canonical virtual-diamond?

choices.1: The std::allocator / allocator_traits pair
choices.2: The std::exception hierarchy
choices.3: The std::vector / std::array container hierarchy
choices.4: iostreams: std::iostream virtually inherits std::istream and std::ostream, which both virtually inherit std::ios (one shared std::ios)

question: What is the practical/HFT guidance on inheritance here?

choices.1: Prefer composition and interface-only inheritance; avoid virtual inheritance on hot paths; use CRTP/mixins for static polymorphism with no vptr/offset overhead
choices.2: Always use virtual inheritance to be safe
choices.3: Replace all interfaces with dynamic_cast checks
choices.4: Use deep stateful multiple inheritance for performance

question: Why is reinterpret_cast between related polymorphic types (across a multiple-inheritance hierarchy) wrong?

choices.1: Because reinterpret_cast cannot be used on pointers at all
choices.2: Because it deletes the vptr during conversion
choices.3: Because it always throws std::bad_cast
choices.4: Because converting to a non-first base requires a pointer-offset adjustment that static_cast performs but reinterpret_cast does not, leaving the pointer pointing at the wrong subobject

question: Why can't constructors be virtual, and what is the usual alternative?

choices.1: They can be virtual, but only in abstract classes
choices.2: Because constructors have no return type to put the vtable in
choices.3: Virtual dispatch needs an existing runtime type, but a constructor is what creates that type, so it can't dispatch to a derived class; use a factory function or a virtual clone() instead
choices.4: Because the standard reserves virtual for destructors only

question: What happens if you call a virtual function from within a base-class constructor (or destructor)?

choices.1: It dispatches to the base version, not the derived one, because the derived part is not yet constructed (or already destroyed)
choices.2: It is a compile error
choices.3: It results in undefined behaviour every time
choices.4: It dispatches to the derived version as usual

question: For Base* p = new Derived(); p->show(); where both override show(int x) with different default args, which default is used?

choices.1: Neither — the call is ambiguous and fails to compile
choices.2: Both defaults are applied and the call takes two arguments
choices.3: The base default (from Base*), even though Derived::show runs — default arguments are statically bound while the call is dynamic
choices.4: The derived default, because Derived::show is the function actually called

question: What does the override keyword do for you?

choices.1: It prevents any further class from overriding the function
choices.2: It marks the function virtual even if the base function is not
choices.3: It forces the function to be called dynamically even through a value object
choices.4: It makes the compiler verify the function actually overrides a base virtual; a signature mismatch (e.g. double vs int, or missing const) becomes an error instead of a silent new overload

question: Why should a polymorphic base class have a virtual destructor?

choices.1: So that deleting a derived object through a base pointer runs the derived destructor; without it the derived destructor may not run, leaking resources (and it is UB)
choices.2: So that the base class can be copied safely
choices.3: So that constructors can be called virtually
choices.4: So that dynamic_cast becomes unnecessary

question: What does a pure virtual function (= 0) make of its class?

choices.1: An abstract class, which cannot be instantiated directly
choices.2: A class with no vtable
choices.3: A final class, which cannot be inherited
choices.4: A trivially copyable class

question: What does the final keyword do?

choices.1: It marks a function as the last one called at program exit
choices.2: It forces a class to be abstract
choices.3: It makes a function non-virtual again
choices.4: Applied to a virtual function it prevents further overriding; applied to a class it prevents any further inheritance

question: What does dynamic_cast<Derived*>(basePtr) require and return on failure?

choices.1: It requires no virtual functions and returns 0 cast to int on failure
choices.2: It requires a polymorphic base (at least one virtual function) and returns nullptr if the object is not actually a Derived
choices.3: It requires the classes to be unrelated and throws on success
choices.4: It works only at compile time and never fails

question: Why does a derived function with a slightly different signature fail to override the base virtual?

choices.1: Because the derived function must be declared before the base one
choices.2: Because overriding ignores parameters entirely and only matches by name
choices.3: C++ matches overrides exactly — differing parameter types, const-ness, or ref-qualifiers make it a separate (overloaded/hidden) function, so polymorphism silently breaks unless override catches it
choices.4: Because only the return type is allowed to differ for an override

question: What is std::any?

choices.1: A reference wrapper that aliases an existing object
choices.2: A type-erased value container that can hold one object of almost any copy-constructible type, forgetting the static type at compile time
choices.3: A container that holds a sequence of heterogeneous values
choices.4: A pointer to a fixed set of alternative types, like a tagged union

question: What does std::any store internally to make type erasure work?

choices.1: A std::variant of every type the program uses
choices.2: A virtual base class subobject for each possible type
choices.3: A void* and nothing else
choices.4: The object (inline or on the heap), runtime type information, and type-specific functions to copy/move/destroy it (a manager/function-pointer table)

question: Why must the type stored in a std::any usually be copy-constructible?

choices.1: Because std::any stores everything on the stack and copying is the only option
choices.2: Because any_cast requires a copy constructor to return by value
choices.3: Because type erasure cannot move objects, only copy them
choices.4: Because std::any is itself copyable and must be able to copy the contained object without knowing its static type — so std::make_unique<int>(5) (move-only) fails

question: What happens with std::any_cast<double>(a) when a holds an int?

choices.1: It succeeds, converting the int to a double
choices.2: It returns nullptr
choices.3: It throws std::bad_any_cast — the value form requires the exact stored type, and int->double convertibility does not help
choices.4: It returns a default-constructed double (0.0)

question: How does the pointer form std::any_cast<int>(&a) differ from the value form?

choices.1: It throws std::bad_any_cast on mismatch, like the value form
choices.2: It returns a pointer to the value on success and nullptr on type mismatch, without throwing
choices.3: It copies the value into a new std::any
choices.4: It always returns a non-null pointer, even for the wrong type

question: What is the small-object optimization in std::any?

choices.1: It shares one buffer across all std::any instances
choices.2: It compresses the stored value to fit in a single byte
choices.3: It caches recently used types to skip type checks
choices.4: Small values may be stored inline inside the std::any object (no heap allocation), while larger values are heap-allocated

question: How does std::any differ from std::variant?

choices.1: std::variant performs no runtime type checking while std::any does
choices.2: They are identical except std::any cannot be empty
choices.3: std::any holds arbitrary copyable types with the type list unknown at compile time and no compile-time exhaustiveness; std::variant holds one of a fixed compile-time list, supports exhaustive visitation, and is often more efficient
choices.4: std::any holds a fixed list of types while std::variant holds arbitrary types

question: How does std::any improve on a raw void*?

choices.1: It also stores the type and knows how to destroy, copy, and runtime-check the object — a safer, owning, runtime-checked 'value of unknown type'
choices.2: It avoids storing the value, only its address
choices.3: It is identical to void* but with a nicer name
choices.4: It is faster because it never allocates

question: What is std::variant, conceptually?

choices.1: A pointer that can point to any of several objects
choices.2: A type-safe tagged union: it stores exactly one value from a fixed set of alternatives and tracks which one is active with a discriminant (index)
choices.3: A type-erased holder for arbitrary copyable types
choices.4: A container that can grow to hold many values of different types

question: What is the core in-memory representation of a std::variant?

choices.1: One separate buffer for each alternative, all live at once
choices.2: Aligned raw storage sized/aligned for the largest alternative, plus an index (tag) recording which alternative is active
choices.3: A std::type_info pointer and a void* to a heap object
choices.4: A heap-allocated node per alternative linked together

question: What is sizeof(std::variant<Ts...>) roughly, and does it allocate?

choices.1: Roughly max(sizeof(Ts)...) (aligned) plus the index; it does not allocate on the heap unless an alternative itself does
choices.2: The sum of sizeof(Ts)... plus the index; always heap-allocated
choices.3: A single pointer; the value always lives on the heap
choices.4: Fixed at 64 bytes regardless of the alternatives

question: How can a std::variant become valueless_by_exception()?

choices.1: If, while changing alternative, the old value's destructor runs but constructing the new value throws, there is no value to hold, so the index is set to a sentinel
choices.2: If you default-construct it without specifying an alternative
choices.3: If you call get<I> with the wrong index
choices.4: Whenever any contained operation throws, regardless of construction

question: How does std::visit(f, v) dispatch to the active alternative?

choices.1: It uses a virtual function call through the variant's vtable
choices.2: It linearly searches the alternatives at runtime comparing typeids
choices.3: Essentially a switch on v.index() that calls f(get<I>(v)) for the active I; for multiple variants it builds an N1*N2*... dispatch table
choices.4: It calls f once per alternative and discards the inactive results

question: How does a variant manage the lifetime of its alternatives when assigned a new one?

choices.1: It zeroes the storage and reconstructs all alternatives
choices.2: It relies on the compiler-generated copy assignment of a union
choices.3: Manually: it destroys the currently active value (explicit destructor via the index), then placement-news the new value in the storage, then updates the index
choices.4: It heap-allocates a fresh object and frees the old pointer

question: Why does get<I>/get<T> involve std::launder when reading the storage?

choices.1: Because launder allocates the object if it does not exist yet
choices.2: Because get<I> must throw if the index is wrong, which launder implements
choices.3: Because the variant does manual lifetime management in raw storage, and std::launder satisfies strict-aliasing / object-lifetime rules when reinterpreting the buffer as T&
choices.4: Because launder converts the bytes from one type to another

question: How does std::variant achieve a trivial destructor when possible?

choices.1: It always has a non-trivial destructor because of the index
choices.2: It can never be trivially destructible
choices.3: It defers destruction to std::visit at scope exit
choices.4: If all alternatives are trivially destructible, the variant's destructor can be trivial too (implementations select trivial vs non-trivial storage bases via traits)

question: Under the Itanium ABI, where is the vptr stored and which classes have one?

choices.1: At the start of the object; a class gets a vptr when it is polymorphic (has at least one virtual function)
choices.2: At the end of the object; every class has one
choices.3: Inside the vtable itself; only derived classes have one
choices.4: In a global table indexed by object address; only abstract classes have one

question: What does a GCC/Itanium vtable contain besides function pointers, and where does the vptr point?

choices.1: The constructor and destructor addresses only
choices.2: Only the function pointers, with the vptr pointing at the last entry
choices.3: A copy of every data member followed by the function pointers
choices.4: Metadata entries precede the function slots: [-2] offset_to_top and [-1] the typeinfo pointer, then [0..] the virtual function pointers; the vptr points at entry [0]

question: What is the offset_to_top field used for?

choices.1: It counts how many virtual functions the class has
choices.2: It records how far this subobject is from the top of the complete object (0 in single inheritance); it supports dynamic_cast and multiple/virtual inheritance
choices.3: It points to the base class's vtable
choices.4: It stores the size of the object in bytes

question: How does a derived class's vtable relate to its base's slot layout when it overrides some virtuals?

choices.1: It reuses the base's slot order: overridden slots point to the derived function, non-overridden slots keep the base function, and newly added virtuals append new slots
choices.2: It removes the base's slots and keeps only overrides
choices.3: It reorders all slots alphabetically by function name
choices.4: It creates an entirely independent layout unrelated to the base

question: In multiple inheritance, how are vptrs arranged in the object?

choices.1: The vptrs are stored together in a separate global array
choices.2: All bases share a single vptr at offset 0
choices.3: Only the first base has a vptr; the others are accessed through it
choices.4: Each base subobject gets its own vptr, each pointing to a different vtable region for that base's view

question: What is a thunk in the context of multiple inheritance?

choices.1: An inlined version of a virtual function
choices.2: A small stub the vtable slot points to that adjusts the this pointer (e.g. this -= offset) before jumping to the real function, needed when calling through a non-first base pointer
choices.3: A pointer to the typeinfo used by dynamic_cast
choices.4: A cached copy of the function result for repeated calls

question: What are the steps of a virtual call under single inheritance (e.g. p->foo())?

choices.1: Compare the object's typeid against all derived types, then call the match
choices.2: Search the vtable linearly for foo by name, then call it
choices.3: Load the object pointer, load the vptr from the object, then make an indirect call to the function pointer at foo's slot — no branching or dynamic type checks
choices.4: Call a runtime helper that performs a dynamic_cast and dispatches

question: If a Derived adds a new virtual function not present in Base, where does it go in Derived's vtable?

choices.1: It replaces Base's first slot
choices.2: It is appended as a new slot after the inherited base slots, which keep their original order
choices.3: It is inserted before all base slots, shifting them down
choices.4: It goes into a separate vtable that Base's vptr also points to

question: According to the note, what is the real cost of a virtual call?

choices.1: The heap allocation needed to build the vtable at runtime
choices.2: Not the one extra pointer load, but everything the compiler and CPU can't do because the callee is unknown: no inlining, no cross-call optimisation, and an indirect branch to predict
choices.3: The mandatory exception-handling setup around each call
choices.4: The cost of constructing a new vtable on every call

question: Why does an unknown virtual target block so much optimisation?

choices.1: Inlining requires a known target; without it the body can't be substituted, so you lose the cascade it enables — constant propagation, dead-code elimination, loop hoisting, strength reduction across the call
choices.2: Because virtual functions cannot be marked const or noexcept
choices.3: Because the vtable lookup forces the stack to be flushed
choices.4: Because virtual functions are always compiled with optimisations disabled

question: What conservative assumptions must the optimiser make around a call to an unknown callee?

choices.1: That it always returns the same value, enabling caching
choices.2: That it may read or write any memory, may throw, and may not return — so values can't be kept in registers across the call and must be reloaded, and memory ops can't be reordered across it
choices.3: That it is pure and side-effect free, allowing aggressive reordering
choices.4: That it never touches memory, so all registers are preserved

question: When can the compiler devirtualise an indirect call into a direct (inlinable) one?

choices.1: Only when the function is marked virtual and override
choices.2: Never — virtual calls are always indirect
choices.3: Only at -O0, where the compiler tracks all types
choices.4: When the dynamic type is known at the call site (local object), the class/method is final, LTO proves a single override, or via a speculative type-guard fallback

question: How does the hardware cost of the indirect branch depend on call-site behaviour?

choices.1: A monomorphic site (one concrete type) is learned by the BTB and is nearly free; a polymorphic/megamorphic site (mixed types) causes frequent target mispredictions, each a ~15-20 cycle flush
choices.2: It is free because the CPU never mispredicts indirect branches
choices.3: It is always a fixed ~20-cycle penalty regardless of types
choices.4: It depends only on the size of the function being called

question: Why does the vptr->vtable->call pointer-chase hurt beyond the predicted branch?

choices.1: It serializes all other threads sharing the cache line
choices.2: It forces a full TLB flush on every call
choices.3: It is a serial dependent chain of loads that can miss cache; the target isn't known until they retire, so the CPU can't prefetch the callee's instructions or run far ahead
choices.4: It doubles the function's stack frame size

question: Which techniques recover a static call target on the hot path?

choices.1: Wrapping each call in a try/catch block
choices.2: Templates/CRTP, std::variant + std::visit, marking classes/methods final, grouping/sorting by concrete type to make sites monomorphic, and LTO
choices.3: Adding more virtual functions so the BTB has more to learn
choices.4: Allocating all polymorphic objects from one pool

question: How does marking a class or method final help performance?

choices.1: It forces the function to be stored in the instruction cache permanently
choices.2: It removes the vptr from the object
choices.3: It converts the function into a macro
choices.4: It tells the compiler no further override can exist, enabling devirtualisation and subsequent inlining

question: What are constrained algorithms in modern C++?

choices.1: Algorithm templates whose valid arguments are restricted with concepts — most often the std::ranges::* algorithms
choices.2: Algorithms that only operate on const containers
choices.3: Algorithms that run under a fixed time budget
choices.4: Algorithms restricted to a single thread

question: Why does std::ranges::sort(lst) fail to compile for a std::list?

choices.1: Because list is not copyable
choices.2: ranges::sort is constrained to random-access ranges, and list only provides bidirectional iterators, so the overload is simply not viable — not a deep template breakage
choices.3: Because list has no begin()/end()
choices.4: Because ranges::sort requires the elements to be integral

question: What is a benefit of encoding requirements as concepts rather than relying on implicit iterator requirements?

choices.1: It allows algorithms to mutate const ranges
choices.2: Diagnostics are tied to the violated concept and overload resolution simply drops non-viable overloads, instead of failing deep inside the algorithm body
choices.3: It removes the need to include <algorithm>
choices.4: The algorithms run faster at runtime

question: What do projections let you do, e.g. std::ranges::sort(people, {}, &Person::age)?

choices.1: Sort only the projected members, leaving the rest of each object behind
choices.2: Run the sort in parallel across members
choices.3: Replace the comparator with a hash function
choices.4: Sort by a member (age) without writing a custom comparator lambda — the projection is applied to each element before comparison

question: What is the call-style advantage of ranges algorithms over classic ones?

choices.1: They automatically parallelise
choices.2: You no longer need to include any headers
choices.3: You can pass a whole range, e.g. std::ranges::find(v, 42), instead of std::find(v.begin(), v.end(), 42)
choices.4: They always return a bool instead of an iterator

question: How do constrained algorithms relate to constrained templates in general?

choices.1: Constrained algorithms predate and inspired constrained templates
choices.2: They are a special case: constrained templates use a requires clause or named concepts, and ranges algorithms apply that idea to iterators, ranges, predicates, projections, and value types
choices.3: Constrained templates are a runtime feature; constrained algorithms are compile-time
choices.4: They are unrelated; constrained algorithms use macros instead of concepts

question: What is the rule-of-thumb distinction between std::find and std::ranges::find?

choices.1: std::find is the older classic style; std::ranges::find is the constrained modern style and is usually the better default
choices.2: std::ranges::find is deprecated in favour of std::find
choices.3: std::find works on views while std::ranges::find does not
choices.4: They are identical aliases

question: When creating a genuinely new view class, which base should it derive from?

choices.1: std::ranges::view_interface (CRTP), which supplies common range members
choices.2: std::ranges::range_adaptor_closure
choices.3: std::ranges::viewable_range
choices.4: std::iterator, the deprecated base

question: Why use std::views::all when storing an incoming range inside your own view?

choices.1: It eagerly copies the range into a new container
choices.2: It converts any range into a random-access range
choices.3: It normalizes the input the standard way: a cheap view wrapper for lvalues and an owning view for moved rvalues
choices.4: It guarantees the range is sorted before storage

question: When should you write a closure object rather than a whole new view type?

choices.1: Always — closures are required for any pipeable adaptor
choices.2: When your adaptor is mostly a reusable composition of existing views; only write your own iterator/view when you can't express the behaviour with existing views
choices.3: Only when the range is infinite
choices.4: Only when targeting C++17 or earlier

question: What design property should custom range adaptors preserve?

choices.1: Eager evaluation, computing all elements up front
choices.2: Laziness — standard range adaptors are built around cheap, lazy views, so avoid eager materialization unless explicitly wanted
choices.3: Thread-safety of every iteration
choices.4: Ownership of the underlying container's elements

question: What is the standard customization hook for pipeable user-defined adaptors, and its version caveat?

choices.1: Overloading operator| as a free function, available since C++11
choices.2: Specializing std::pipeable, a C++20 trait
choices.3: Inheriting from std::ranges::view, available since C++14
choices.4: The std::ranges::range_adaptor_closure machinery (cleanly available in C++23+); in plain C++20 the portable route is often call-style-only adaptors or a library like range-v3

question: What are the essential pieces of a minimal custom view?

choices.1: Deriving from view_interface, storing the underlying range as a view, and defining begin()/end() plus an iterator implementing the traversal
choices.2: A heap-allocated buffer holding a copy of all elements
choices.3: A virtual base class and a vtable of traversal functions
choices.4: A global registry of all active views

question: What practical rule does the note give before writing a custom view?

choices.1: Start by deriving from std::vector
choices.2: First ask 'can I express this as existing views?'; only build your own iterator/view when the answer is no
choices.3: Always write a custom view for maximum control
choices.4: Never write custom views; use loops instead

question: What is the mental model for ranges, views, and range adaptors?

choices.1: A range is a view, a view is an adaptor, and an adaptor is a container
choices.2: A range is anything iterable, a view is a lightweight usually non-owning range, and an adaptor transforms one range into another (usually producing a view)
choices.3: A range owns data, a view copies it, and an adaptor sorts it
choices.4: All three are synonyms for a container

question: What does it mean that views are lazy?

choices.1: They cache all results on construction for fast reuse
choices.2: They defer destruction until program exit
choices.3: They run on a background thread
choices.4: They compute values on demand: building a pipeline does no work until you actually iterate it

question: How does a view relate to the elements it presents?

choices.1: It moves the elements out of the source range
choices.2: It usually does not own them — it stores just enough to iterate and acts like a window/recipe over existing data, not storage
choices.3: It always owns a private copy of the elements
choices.4: It reference-counts the elements

question: What is the biggest practical pitfall of views?

choices.1: Lifetime: because many views are non-owning, returning a view over a local container leaves it dangling once the underlying range is destroyed
choices.2: They always allocate a new container, wasting memory
choices.3: They force eager evaluation, blocking early exit
choices.4: They cannot be composed with other views

question: In auto r = v | std::views::filter(pred) | std::views::transform(fn);, what is r?

choices.1: A function object that must be called to run
choices.2: A copy of v with the elements modified in place
choices.3: A new std::vector holding the filtered, transformed results
choices.4: A lazy view pipeline over v — no intermediate containers are created and nothing is computed until r is iterated

question: What do std::views::keys and std::views::values yield over a range of pair-like elements?

choices.1: keys yields unique elements, values yields duplicates
choices.2: keys yields the second element, values yields the first
choices.3: keys yields the first element of each pair, values yields the second
choices.4: Both yield the whole pair

question: When is a plain container or explicit loop preferable to a view pipeline?

choices.1: Always — views are never worth using
