choices.4: Constructors, deduction guides (implicit and explicit), and aggregate deduction rules — not the class template parameters by themselves

question: Why does 'X x;' fail to compile for template<typename T> struct X { X(){} };?

choices.1: T defaults to void, which is ill-formed
choices.2: There is no constructor argument to deduce T from, so deduction fails — you must write X<int> x or provide a deduction guide
choices.3: X must be declared final first
choices.4: Default constructors are not allowed in class templates

question: What does a user-defined deduction guide like 'Holder(const char*) -> Holder<std::string>;' accomplish?

choices.1: It disables CTAD for Holder
choices.2: It converts const char* to std::string at runtime only
choices.3: It defines a new constructor for Holder
choices.4: It controls the deduced class type, e.g. making Holder("hello") deduce Holder<std::string> instead of Holder<const char*>

question: Why does 'Point p{1, 2.5};' usually fail for template<typename T> struct Point { T x; T y; };?

choices.1: Aggregate CTAD tries to deduce one T from both members: 1 gives int and 2.5 gives double, a conflict — CTAD does not invent a common type without a guide
choices.2: It succeeds and deduces Point<double>
choices.3: Brace initialization is illegal for templates
choices.4: Aggregates can never use CTAD

question: How does CTAD differ from auto?

choices.1: CTAD deduces expression types while auto deduces template arguments
choices.2: They are the same feature with two spellings
choices.3: auto deduces the type of an expression; CTAD deduces a class template's arguments from a constructor-style initialization — related in spirit but different features
choices.4: auto works only on class templates; CTAD only on scalars

question: For 'template<typename T> void f(T x);' called with a const int, what is T deduced as, and why?

choices.1: T = int&, because the argument is an lvalue
choices.2: Deduction fails for const arguments
choices.3: T = const int, because const is preserved
choices.4: T = int — passing by value drops top-level const (and references), so the cv-qualifier is stripped

question: For 'template<typename T> void f(T& x);', how does an array argument like int arr[10] deduce T?

choices.1: T = int[10] — binding by reference prevents array-to-pointer decay, so the full array type is preserved
choices.2: T = int*, because arrays always decay
choices.3: Deduction fails because arrays can't bind to references
choices.4: T = int, dropping the bound

question: For a forwarding reference 'template<typename T> void f(T&& x);', what is T for an lvalue vs an rvalue argument?

choices.1: An lvalue deduces a plain type and an rvalue deduces a reference
choices.2: T&& is always an rvalue reference regardless of the argument
choices.3: Both deduce T as the plain type
choices.4: An lvalue deduces T = (the type)& (a reference), collapsing the parameter to an lvalue reference; an rvalue deduces T = the plain type, giving an rvalue reference

question: Why does 'f(1, 2.0)' fail for 'template<typename T> void f(T a, T b);'?

choices.1: Because mixing int and double is a runtime error
choices.2: Because 2.0 cannot be deduced at all
choices.3: The compiler must deduce one T from both arguments: 1 gives int, 2.0 gives double — a conflict, and deduction does not apply arbitrary conversions to reconcile them
choices.4: Because f can only take one argument

question: Why is 'template<typename T> void f(typename T::value_type x);' a non-deduced context?

choices.1: Because T::value_type is a function, not a type
choices.2: Because typename is not allowed in parameters
choices.3: The argument's type (e.g. int) cannot be worked backward to determine T, since many types could have value_type = int; you must supply T explicitly
choices.4: Because value_type is always ambiguous

question: When do implicit conversions apply relative to template argument deduction?

choices.1: Conversions never apply to template functions
choices.2: Conversions apply only to the return type
choices.3: Deduction itself is strict (no arbitrary conversions); conversions apply afterward, once the template arguments are known (e.g. f<double>(1) converts int to double)
choices.4: Conversions happen during deduction to make arguments match

question: What is a dependent name?

choices.1: A name with internal linkage
choices.2: A name whose meaning depends on a template parameter (e.g. T::value_type), so it cannot be fully resolved until the template arguments are substituted
choices.3: A name that depends on a runtime value
choices.4: Any name declared inside a template

question: What is two-phase lookup?

choices.1: The compiler parses templates twice to catch syntax errors
choices.2: Non-dependent names are looked up at template definition time (phase 1); dependent names are resolved later at instantiation time (phase 2)
choices.3: Names are looked up once in the header and once in the source file
choices.4: Overloads are resolved before deduction and again after

question: Why does an unqualified call to an inherited member 'hello()' fail in a Derived<T> : Base<T>?

choices.1: Base<T> is a dependent base class, so phase-1 unqualified lookup does not consider its members; fix with this->hello(), Base<T>::hello(), or a using-declaration
choices.2: Because inheritance is not allowed in templates
choices.3: Because hello() is private in Base
choices.4: Because Base<T> has not been instantiated yet at runtime

question: Why must you write 'typename T::value_type x;' rather than 'T::value_type x;'?

choices.1: T::value_type is a dependent qualified name, and before instantiation the compiler can't tell if it is a type or a value; typename disambiguates it as a type so the statement parses
choices.2: Because T must be fully defined first
choices.3: typename is required for all member accesses
choices.4: Because value_type is a reserved keyword

question: When is the disambiguating keyword 'template' needed, as in 'obj.template foo<int>();'?

choices.1: Only when foo is a static member
choices.2: Whenever you call any member function of a template type
choices.3: When foo is a dependent member template; without 'template' the compiler may parse foo<int> as a less-than comparison rather than a template call
choices.4: Only inside a deduction guide

question: What are the three common template lookup disambiguators?

choices.1: typename (a dependent qualified name is a type), template (a dependent qualified name is a template), and this-> / explicit qualification (find members from a dependent base)
choices.2: static, extern, and inline
choices.3: public, protected, and private
choices.4: const, volatile, and mutable

question: What are the three main jobs of '...' with variadic templates (plus the separate C-style use)?

choices.1: Only declare a parameter pack
choices.2: Declare a pack (template<typename... Ts>), expand a pack (f(args...)), and fold a pack ((... + args)); separately, void f(...) is C-style varargs
choices.3: Only expand a pack into a function call
choices.4: Comment out the rest of a parameter list

question: In 'g(h(args)...)' with args = a, b, c, what does the expansion produce?

choices.1: g(h(a)), g(h(b)), g(h(c)) as three separate statements
choices.2: h(g(a), g(b), g(c))
choices.3: g(h(a, b, c)) — the whole pack is passed to one h call
choices.4: g(h(a), h(b), h(c)) — the ellipsis repeats the pattern h(args) for each pack element

question: What does sizeof...(args) give?

choices.1: The total size in bytes of all arguments
choices.2: The number of elements in the parameter pack
choices.3: The size of the largest element
choices.4: The index of the last element

question: For args = 10, 3, 2, how do the unary left fold (... - args) and unary right fold (args - ...) differ?

choices.1: Left fold is ((10 - 3) - 2) = 5; right fold is 10 - (3 - 2) = 9 — fold direction matters for non-associative operators
choices.2: Left fold is 9 and right fold is 5
choices.3: Both evaluate to 9
choices.4: Both evaluate to 5

question: Why use a binary fold like (0 + ... + args) instead of the unary (args + ...)?

choices.1: The unary form cannot use the + operator
choices.2: The binary form is faster at runtime
choices.3: The binary form avoids template instantiation
choices.4: The binary form supplies an initial/identity value, so it works even when the pack is empty (the result starts from 0)

question: How does a comma fold like '((std::cout << args << ' '), ...);' work?

choices.1: It prints only the first argument
choices.2: It uses the comma operator to perform a side effect (print) for each element of the pack
choices.3: It is invalid syntax
choices.4: It sums the arguments and prints the total

question: How do C-style varargs (void f(...)) and variadic templates fundamentally differ?

choices.1: They are identical features with different syntax
choices.2: Varargs are type-safe while variadic templates are not
choices.3: C-style varargs are not type-safe and need manual va_list extraction with no per-argument type info; variadic templates keep every argument's type in the type system and support pack expansion and folds
choices.4: Variadic templates are runtime while varargs are compile-time

question: What do variadic templates let a template accept?

choices.1: Exactly two template arguments
choices.2: Only runtime arguments, not compile-time ones
choices.3: Only arguments of the same type
choices.4: Any number of template (or function) arguments, via a parameter pack like template<typename... Ts>

question: What limitation did variadic templates solve compared to older fixed-arity templates?

choices.1: They allowed templates to be called at runtime
choices.2: They removed the need for the typename keyword
choices.3: They made templates compile faster
choices.4: You no longer need separate MyTupleN templates for each argument count — one template<typename... Ts> works for all counts

question: Why are variadic templates safer than C-style variadic functions?

choices.1: They keep every argument's type in the type system (full compile-time type checking), unlike va_list-based varargs with weak type checking
choices.2: They forbid pointers as arguments
choices.3: They automatically validate printf format strings
choices.4: They run inside a sandbox at runtime

question: Which standard library features depend on variadic templates?

choices.1: std::tuple<Ts...>, std::variant<Ts...>, std::make_unique/make_shared perfect-forwarding helpers, and type traits over many types
choices.2: std::string and std::string_view
choices.3: Only the C standard library
choices.4: std::vector and std::array only

question: How does a recursive variadic print 'void print(First first, Rest... rest)' process its arguments?

choices.1: It prints all arguments in a single non-recursive call
choices.2: It peels off one argument at a time (printing first, then calling print(rest...)) until a base-case overload handles the end
choices.3: It prints them in reverse order
choices.4: It requires the arguments to be sorted

question: What does MOCK_METHOD(bool, Save, (int id, const std::string& value), (override)); do?

choices.1: It defines a real implementation that saves to disk
choices.2: It generates a mocked implementation of the virtual Save method; the fourth argument holds specifiers like override/const/noexcept
choices.3: It registers Save as a free function
choices.4: It asserts that Save is called exactly once

question: What is the difference between EXPECT_CALL and ON_CALL?

choices.1: ON_CALL requires the call; EXPECT_CALL sets defaults
choices.2: EXPECT_CALL is for matchers; ON_CALL is for actions
choices.3: They are identical aliases
choices.4: EXPECT_CALL requires a call to happen and verifies it; ON_CALL only sets default behavior without requiring the call

question: What is the difference between NiceMock<T>, NaggyMock<T>, and StrictMock<T>?

choices.1: They set how many times each method may be called
choices.2: They control the return type of the mock
choices.3: NiceMock is fastest, NaggyMock is slowest, StrictMock is safest at runtime
choices.4: NiceMock suppresses warnings for uninteresting calls, NaggyMock (the default) warns on them, and StrictMock treats uninteresting calls as test failures

question: How do you make a mock write an out-parameter and return a value, e.g. Read(int* out)?

choices.1: Use EXPECT_CALL twice for the same method
choices.2: Use an action like DoAll(SetArgPointee<0>(123), Return(true)) inside WillOnce
choices.3: Out parameters cannot be mocked
choices.4: Return a std::pair from the method

question: How does gMock determine a call's cardinality when you don't specify Times()?

choices.1: It infers it from the number of WillOnce / WillRepeatedly clauses (defaulting to once if none)
choices.2: It allows any number of calls by default
choices.3: It throws an error requiring an explicit Times()
choices.4: It always requires exactly zero calls

question: How do you enforce that mock calls happen in a specific order?

choices.1: Set Times(1) on each call
choices.2: gMock cannot verify call ordering
choices.3: Use an InSequence object (or named Sequence objects with .InSequence(...)) so the expectations must occur in the declared order
choices.4: List the EXPECT_CALLs alphabetically

question: What is the difference between EXPECT_* and ASSERT_* macros?

choices.1: EXPECT_* records a non-fatal failure and lets the test continue; ASSERT_* records a fatal failure that stops the current function
choices.2: ASSERT_* is non-fatal; EXPECT_* is fatal
choices.3: They are identical
choices.4: EXPECT_* runs at compile time; ASSERT_* at runtime

question: What does a test fixture (a class deriving from ::testing::Test, used with TEST_F) provide?

choices.1: A way to run tests in parallel automatically
choices.2: Mock objects with no manual definition
choices.3: A single setup that runs once for the whole program
choices.4: Shared setup/teardown via SetUp()/TearDown() that run before and after each individual test, plus shared members

question: What does SetUpTestSuite()/TearDownTestSuite() do versus SetUp()/TearDown()?

choices.1: They are only for parameterized tests
choices.2: They run per test, like SetUp/TearDown
choices.3: They replace the main() function
choices.4: The TestSuite versions run once before/after all tests in the suite (for expensive shared resources); SetUp/TearDown run per individual test

question: What are value-parameterized tests (TEST_P + INSTANTIATE_TEST_SUITE_P) for?

choices.1: Running the same test over multiple types
choices.2: Disabling tests by parameter
choices.3: Running the same test logic over multiple input values, accessed via GetParam() (with generators like Values, ValuesIn, Range)
choices.4: Mocking parameterized constructors

question: How do you temporarily disable a test in GoogleTest while keeping it compiled?

choices.1: Prefix its name with DISABLED_ (e.g. TEST(Suite, DISABLED_Broken)); run it later with --gtest_also_run_disabled_tests
choices.2: Delete the TEST macro and keep the body
choices.3: Add .Skip() to the assertion
choices.4: Wrap it in #if 0 / #endif

question: What does the EXPECT_THAT macro enable, and where do its matchers come from?

choices.1: Richer assertions using gMock matchers (e.g. ElementsAre, HasSubstr, AllOf), included via <gmock/gmock.h>
choices.2: Compile-time static assertions
choices.3: Parameterized test instantiation
choices.4: Floating-point comparisons only

question: Which std::chrono clock should you use to measure elapsed time, and why?

choices.1: std::chrono::steady_clock — it is monotonic (cannot go backwards) and advances at a steady rate, ideal for durations/timeouts/benchmarking
choices.2: std::chrono::high_resolution_clock, because it is always the most precise and monotonic
choices.3: std::chrono::system_clock, because it tracks calendar time
choices.4: std::chrono::utc_clock, because it is leap-second aware

question: What does std::chrono::system_clock represent, and what is its caveat?

choices.1: International Atomic Time with no leap seconds
choices.2: A filesystem-only timestamp clock
choices.3: System-wide wall-clock (calendar) time, counting Unix time since 1970-01-01 UTC (excluding leap seconds) — but it can be adjusted by the OS/NTP/user and may jump forwards or backwards
choices.4: A guaranteed-monotonic clock that never jumps

question: Why should you be cautious using std::chrono::high_resolution_clock for elapsed time?

choices.1: It cannot produce a time_point
choices.2: It may simply be an alias for system_clock or steady_clock, and its is_steady is implementation-specific — so it is not guaranteed monotonic; prefer steady_clock
choices.3: It is only available in C++20
choices.4: It is always slower than system_clock

question: What distinguishes std::chrono::utc_clock from system_clock's sys_time?

choices.1: utc_clock is for filesystem timestamps
choices.2: utc_clock is monotonic while system_clock is not
choices.3: utc_clock has no epoch
choices.4: utc_clock counts time including leap seconds since 1970-01-01 UTC, whereas sys_time excludes leap seconds

question: What is std::chrono::file_clock used for?

choices.1: Filesystem timestamps such as std::filesystem::last_write_time; its epoch is unspecified, so it should not be treated as Unix time
choices.2: Wall-clock logging with leap seconds
choices.3: Measuring elapsed CPU time
choices.4: GPS satellite timestamps

question: What does std::chrono::duration<Rep, Period> represent?

choices.1: A clock with an epoch and now() method
choices.2: A specific point on a clock's timeline
choices.3: A length of time as count x tick period, where Rep is the numeric storage type and Period is the tick length as a rational fraction of seconds
choices.4: A calendar date and time

question: What does .count() return on a duration, and what is the catch?

choices.1: A floating-point value in seconds
choices.2: The number of nanoseconds, normalized
choices.3: Always the number of seconds, regardless of unit
choices.4: The raw number of ticks in the duration's own unit — it does not automatically mean seconds (e.g. seconds{2}.count() and milliseconds{2}.count() both return 2 but mean different things)

question: Why does 'std::chrono::seconds s = ms;' (from milliseconds) fail to compile while 'milliseconds ms = s;' (from seconds) succeeds?

choices.1: Both directions are always allowed
choices.2: Neither direction compiles without duration_cast
choices.3: Implicit duration conversion is only allowed when it is exact; seconds->milliseconds loses no precision, but milliseconds->integer seconds would truncate (e.g. 1500ms = 1.5s)
choices.4: seconds cannot be assigned from any other duration

question: What does std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::seconds>(milliseconds{1999}) yield, and how does it round?

choices.1: It is a compile error
choices.2: 2 seconds — it rounds to nearest
choices.3: 1.999 seconds — it preserves the fraction
choices.4: 1 second — duration_cast truncates toward zero

question: What is the cleanest way to get elapsed seconds as a fractional double, e.g. 1500ms -> 1.5?

choices.1: Call d.count() directly, which is always in seconds
choices.2: Use duration_cast<seconds>(d).count(), which keeps the fraction
choices.3: There is no way to get a fractional value from a duration
choices.4: Use a floating-point duration: std::chrono::duration<double>(d).count() (where duration<double> means seconds stored as a double)

question: duration_cast truncates. What do std::chrono::floor/ceil/round provide?

choices.1: They only work on floating-point durations
choices.2: They are aliases for duration_cast
choices.3: Conversions that floor, ceil, or round to the target duration instead of truncating toward zero (e.g. round<seconds>(1500ms) = 2s)
choices.4: They convert durations to time_points

question: What does a std::chrono::time_point represent?

choices.1: A calendar struct with year/month/day fields
choices.2: A clock object with a now() method
choices.3: A specific point on a clock's timeline — conceptually clock epoch + a duration since epoch (it stores that duration)
choices.4: A length of time, like 5 seconds

question: What does subtracting two time_points from the same clock give?

choices.1: A bool indicating which is earlier
choices.2: Another time_point
choices.3: A raw integer of nanoseconds
choices.4: A duration (the elapsed time between them), e.g. end - start

question: Why can't you subtract a system_clock::time_point from a steady_clock::time_point?

choices.1: Because system_clock time points are unsigned
choices.2: They are points on different timelines (different clocks), so mixing them has no meaningful result and is ill-formed
choices.3: You can — it returns the offset between the two epochs
choices.4: Because steady_clock has no time_since_epoch()

question: What does time_since_epoch() return, and how do you get a primitive number from it?

choices.1: It returns another time_point at the epoch
choices.2: It returns a calendar date string
choices.3: It returns a duration (from the clock's epoch to the time_point); call .count() (often via duration_cast or duration<double>) to get a primitive value
choices.4: It returns an int64 of nanoseconds directly

question: What does adding a duration to a time_point produce (e.g. now + seconds{5})?

choices.1: A bool indicating whether the deadline passed
choices.2: A new time_point shifted by that duration — commonly used to build a deadline
choices.3: An error, since time_points are immutable
choices.4: A duration representing the offset

question: Why is steady_clock::now().time_since_epoch() usually NOT meaningful as a timestamp, while system_clock's is?

choices.1: steady_clock has no epoch at all, so it returns 0
choices.2: steady_clock time points cannot call time_since_epoch()
choices.3: system_clock's value is also meaningless; both are only for differences
choices.4: steady_clock's epoch is unspecified (e.g. time since boot) so the absolute value isn't a calendar time — it is only meaningful as a difference; system_clock maps to wall-clock Unix time

question: What kinds of bugs does AddressSanitizer (ASan) detect, and at what cost?

choices.1: Undefined behavior like signed overflow, with no instrumentation
choices.2: Heap/stack buffer overflows, use-after-free, use-after-scope, double delete, and out-of-bounds accesses — with roughly 2x slowdown, enabled via -fsanitize=address
choices.3: Data races and deadlocks, with no runtime overhead
choices.4: Use of uninitialized memory, with about 10x slowdown

question: How does ASan's shadow memory work?

choices.1: It stores a full copy of every byte for comparison
choices.2: It hashes each pointer into a table of recent accesses
choices.3: For every 8 bytes of application memory it keeps 1 shadow byte (shadow = (addr>>3)+offset); 0 means fully addressable, 1-7 means partially valid, and negative means poisoned/unaddressable — giving constant-time checks
choices.4: It keeps one shadow byte per byte of memory

question: What are redzones in ASan?

choices.1: Inaccessible padding placed around allocations, so an out-of-bounds access quickly hits a poisoned shadow byte
choices.2: Regions where atomics are disallowed
choices.3: Reserved high-priority memory for the sanitizer runtime
choices.4: Cache lines marked read-only to detect writes

question: How does ASan detect use-after-free?

choices.1: It zeroes freed memory and checks for non-zero reads
choices.2: It scans the heap periodically for dangling pointers
choices.3: It relies on the OS to fault on any freed page
choices.4: It overrides malloc/free: freed blocks are poisoned and held in a quarantine (not immediately returned to the system), so accessing freed memory triggers an error right away

question: Why can ASan miss an out-of-bounds access that stays within the same allocation?

choices.1: ASan disables checks for arrays larger than 8 elements
choices.2: ASan detects overflows by placing redzones around allocations; if the access stays inside the same malloc'd chunk without crossing into a redzone (e.g. two objects packed back-to-back), there is no poisoned byte to trip
choices.3: ASan ignores stack allocations entirely
choices.4: ASan only checks writes, never reads

question: Which bug classes does ASan NOT detect (needing other tools)?

choices.1: Use of uninitialized memory (MemorySanitizer's job) and data races (ThreadSanitizer's job)
choices.2: Out-of-bounds reads and writes
choices.3: Double frees and stack-buffer overflows
choices.4: Heap buffer overflows and use-after-free

question: How does Linux perf fundamentally operate?

choices.1: It is a userspace client of the kernel's perf events subsystem (via perf_event_open()), which programs PMUs / tracepoints and streams counts or samples into an mmap'd ring buffer perf reads
choices.2: It is a kernel module that patches the application binary at load time
choices.3: It runs entirely in userspace by single-stepping the program
choices.4: It uses ptrace to intercept every instruction

question: What is the difference between perf's counting and sampling modes?

choices.1: Counting is for memory and sampling is for CPU only
choices.2: Counting samples call stacks; sampling totals event counts
choices.3: Counting (perf stat) accumulates event counts like cycles/instructions; sampling (perf record/top) raises an interrupt every N events (or a target frequency) and records a sample with IP, call stack, PID/TID, etc.
choices.4: Both modes record full instruction traces

question: What happens when you ask perf stat to count more events than there are hardware counters?

choices.1: It runs the program once per event sequentially
choices.2: It silently drops the extra events with no scaling
choices.3: The kernel time-multiplexes (timeshares) the events across the limited counters and returns scaled counts based on active time
choices.4: It refuses to run and reports an error

question: What is 'skid' in perf sampling, and how is it reduced?

choices.1: Skid is counter overflow; raising the period removes it
choices.2: The sampled instruction pointer can land a few instructions past the actual event; precise events (e.g. cycles:pp) use PEBS (Intel) or IBS (AMD) to reduce it
choices.3: Skid is the time lost to context switches; pinning CPUs removes it
choices.4: Skid is ring-buffer overrun; a bigger buffer removes it

question: What are perf's main call-graph unwinding methods?

choices.1: Source-level tracing via the compiler
choices.2: ptrace-based stack walking only
choices.3: Frame-pointer (fast, needs -fno-omit-frame-pointer), DWARF (works without frame pointers but heavier), and LBR (hardware last-branch records)
choices.4: Only DWARF; frame pointers are never used

question: What controls whether perf can do kernel/system-wide profiling?

choices.1: An environment variable PERF_ALLOW_KERNEL
choices.2: The SUID bit on the perf binary alone
choices.3: /proc/sys/kernel/perf_event_paranoid — higher values restrict to own user-space, while lower/negative values or CAP_SYS_ADMIN allow kernel and system-wide events
choices.4: Nothing; perf always has full access

question: What is the difference between 'perf stat' and 'perf record' for finding what's slow?

choices.1: perf stat gives a one-run counter summary (cycles, instructions, cache-misses, branches); perf record samples call stacks to find hot functions, writing perf.data for perf report
choices.2: Both produce identical interactive reports
choices.3: perf stat is for memory and perf record is for syscalls
choices.4: perf stat samples call stacks; perf record only counts cache misses

question: What does 'perf top' provide?

choices.1: A recording of scheduler events
choices.2: A one-shot summary of cache-miss ratios
choices.3: Live, system-wide hotspots — like top but showing which functions are hot in real time
choices.4: A microbenchmark of futex wakeups

question: What is 'perf c2c' used for?

choices.1: Counting context switches per CPU
choices.2: Listing all available PMU events
choices.3: Cache-to-cache (false sharing) analysis — showing cache lines bouncing between cores/sockets
choices.4: Tracing syscall latencies like strace

question: What does 'perf mem record' / 'perf mem report' break down?

choices.1: Branch mispredictions per loop
choices.2: The number of page faults per function
choices.3: Memory load/store latency samples by source — L1/L2/LLC/DRAM — to show where cache misses come from
choices.4: Lock contention between threads

question: What build flags does the note recommend for good perf call stacks?

choices.1: -fsanitize=address
choices.2: -flto only
choices.3: -O0 with no debug info
choices.4: -O2 -g -fno-omit-frame-pointer (or use --call-graph dwarf); on x86 frame pointers are cheaper than DWARF unwinding

question: What does 'perf stat -r 5 -- ./app' do?

choices.1: Records 5 seconds of system-wide samples
choices.2: Repeats the program until 5 cache misses occur
choices.3: Counts only the 5 most frequent events
choices.4: Runs the app 5 times and reports the mean and standard deviation, useful for stability/repeatability

question: How do vector clocks decide whether two events are a data race?

choices.1: If one event's vector clock is <= the other's component-wise, they are ordered by happens-before; if neither is <= the other they are concurrent — and concurrent conflicting accesses (same variable, >=1 write) are a race
choices.2: If the events ran on different CPUs, they race
choices.3: If both events have the same timestamp, they race
choices.4: If the sum of one clock exceeds the other's, they race

question: In the vector-clock rules, what does a thread do on a local (non-sync) event?

choices.1: It increments every component
choices.2: It resets its clock to zero
choices.3: It increments its own component of the vector clock
choices.4: It copies the other thread's clock

question: What is the vector-clock rule on synchronization (e.g. receiving a message or acquiring a lock)?

choices.1: Replace the local clock entirely with the sender's
choices.2: Add the two clocks component-wise
choices.3: Decrement the sender's component
choices.4: Take the component-wise max of the local clock and the sender's clock, then increment the receiver's own component

question: Two threads write x with no synchronization: T1 reaches [1,0] and T2 reaches [0,1]. What does the vector-clock check conclude?

choices.1: Neither [1,0] <= [0,1] nor [0,1] <= [1,0], so the writes are concurrent — a write-write data race on x
choices.2: [1,0] <= [0,1], so T1 happens-before T2 and there is no race
choices.3: They are equal, so there is no race
choices.4: The clocks are comparable, so it is a benign race

question: After T1 writes x ([1,0]) then signals T2, T2's clock becomes [1,1] before it writes x. Why is there no race?

choices.1: Because writes after a signal are always benign
choices.2: Because both writes have the same value
choices.3: Because T2's clock is larger in total, so it wins
choices.4: W(x) = [1,0] <= T2's clock [1,1] component-wise, so T1's write happens-before T2's write — they are ordered

question: What is the one-line intuition for vector-clock race detection?

choices.1: A race is a pair of conflicting accesses whose vector timestamps are incomparable — i.e. there is no happens-before order between them
choices.2: A race is detected whenever a lock is not held
choices.3: A race is two accesses with equal vector clocks
choices.4: A race is any two accesses to the same address

question: What is ThreadSanitizer (TSan)?

choices.1: A static analyzer that proves a program is race-free at compile time
choices.2: A dynamic happens-before data-race detector: the compiler instruments memory accesses and synchronization, and the runtime flags unordered conflicting accesses
choices.3: A memory-leak detector based on reference counting
choices.4: A profiler that measures lock contention

question: Conceptually, how does TSan instrument 'x = 42;'?

choices.1: It logs the value 42 to a trace file
choices.2: It wraps the write in a mutex automatically
choices.3: It becomes __tsan_write4(&x); x = 42; — the runtime then checks whether another recent access to those bytes from another thread (with >=1 write) is not ordered by happens-before
choices.4: It replaces the write with an atomic store

question: What does a TSan shadow slot store for a memory cell?

choices.1: The complete vector clock of every thread
choices.2: A full copy of the 8 bytes of program memory
choices.3: Only a single dirty bit per cache line
choices.4: Metadata about a recent access — thread id, epoch, read/write and atomic bits, and a byte mask of which bytes were touched — not the stored value

question: Why does TSan store compact epochs in shadow memory but keep full vector clocks on threads/sync objects?

choices.1: Storing a full vector clock per memory location would scale with the number of threads for every byte; instead it keeps compact {thread id, epoch} per location and compares against the current thread's full vector clock (a FastTrack-style optimization)
choices.2: Because threads do not need clocks, only memory does
choices.3: Because epochs are more accurate than vector clocks
choices.4: Because vector clocks cannot be stored in shadow memory at all

question: What is TSan's race condition, stated precisely?

choices.1: Two accesses to the same address from the same thread
choices.2: Two accesses not protected by the same lock
choices.3: Same bytes AND different threads AND at least one write AND not both safely atomic AND current_thread_clock[old_thread] < old_epoch
choices.4: Any two writes anywhere in the program

question: How does TSan's approach differ from a 'lockset' detector?

choices.1: TSan ignores synchronization entirely
choices.2: TSan asks whether the accesses are ordered by happens-before (understanding mutexes, thread create/join, condition variables, atomics) rather than whether both were protected by the same lock — a lock is just one way to create a happens-before edge
choices.3: TSan requires every access to hold the same lock
choices.4: TSan only inspects lock acquisition order

question: What does UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer (UBSan) actually do?

choices.1: It rewrites UB into well-defined behavior automatically
choices.2: It tracks shadow memory to catch buffer overflows
choices.3: It refuses to compile any code containing UB
choices.4: It does not prevent the invalid operation — it instruments UB-prone operations to detect UB, print a diagnostic, and continue (or abort if configured); enabled with -fsanitize=undefined

question: How does UBSan instrument something like 'int z = a / b;'?

choices.1: It inserts a check before the operation, e.g. if (b == 0) __ubsan_handle_divrem_overflow(...); int z = a / b;
choices.2: It wraps the division in a try/catch block
choices.3: It logs the operands without any check
choices.4: It replaces the division with a library call that never divides by zero

question: Which of these does UBSan detect?

choices.1: Data races between threads
choices.2: Memory leaks
choices.3: Heap buffer overflows and use-after-free
choices.4: Signed integer overflow, misaligned/bad pointer casts (type mismatch), out-of-bounds array indexing, and shift overflow

question: Which bug class does UBSan NOT detect (it's another tool's job)?

choices.1: Buffer overflow — that is AddressSanitizer's job; UBSan does not track memory or shadow state
choices.2: Shift-out-of-bounds
choices.3: Signed integer overflow
choices.4: Misaligned pointer access

question: Why can UBSan produce false negatives?

choices.1: Because it disables itself after the first error
choices.2: If the optimizer evaluates or optimizes the UB away at compile time, UBSan never sees it at runtime; it also misses UB in inline assembly, syscalls, or custom allocators where no instrumentation is inserted
choices.3: Because it only runs on debug builds
choices.4: Because it cannot read .rodata metadata

question: How does UBSan produce a readable diagnostic like 'signed integer overflow: 2147483647 + 1 cannot be represented'?

choices.1: It reconstructs the message from the stack trace alone
choices.2: It stores metadata (source filename, line/column, and the expected constraint) in .rodata, and the runtime handler uses that pointer to print the message
choices.3: It parses the source file at runtime
choices.4: It reads DWARF debug info from a separate server

question: What is static_cast used for, and what can it NOT do?

choices.1: Reinterpreting the raw bit pattern of unrelated pointer types
choices.2: Compile-time conversions between compatible types (numeric conversions, upcasting, void* to T*); it cannot cast away const or volatile
choices.3: Removing const-ness from a variable
choices.4: Runtime-checked downcasting in polymorphic hierarchies

question: What is unique about const_cast, and what is its danger?

choices.1: It reinterprets the binary representation of a type
choices.2: It performs compile-time numeric conversions
choices.3: It checks the dynamic type at runtime
choices.4: It is the only cast that can add or remove const/volatile; modifying a truly const object through it is undefined behaviour (safe only if the original object was not declared const)

question: What does reinterpret_cast do?

choices.1: It safely downcasts within a polymorphic class hierarchy
choices.2: It reinterprets the binary representation of one type as another (e.g. between unrelated pointer types); it is not type-safe and is for low-level/platform code
choices.3: It performs implicit numeric promotions only
choices.4: It strips const and nothing else

question: What are the requirements and failure modes of dynamic_cast?

choices.1: It safely downcasts (Base* -> Derived*) using runtime type info; the base must be polymorphic (>=1 virtual function); it returns nullptr for a failed pointer cast and throws std::bad_cast for a failed reference cast
choices.2: It works on any types and never fails
choices.3: It requires the base to be non-polymorphic
choices.4: It is resolved entirely at compile time

question: How does a C-style cast (T)expr choose what to do, and why is it discouraged?

choices.1: It tries const_cast, static_cast, static_cast+const_cast, reinterpret_cast, then reinterpret_cast+const_cast, picking the first that compiles — which hides intent and can silently become an unsafe reinterpret_cast
choices.2: It performs a runtime-checked conversion like dynamic_cast
choices.3: It always behaves exactly like static_cast
choices.4: It only ever removes const-ness

question: Which cast should you use to safely downcast a Base* to a Derived* in a polymorphic hierarchy?

choices.1: static_cast — it is the safest cast
choices.2: reinterpret_cast — it handles related pointer types
choices.3: const_cast — it adjusts the pointer type
choices.4: dynamic_cast — it checks the actual object type at runtime

question: How do undefined, unspecified, and implementation-defined behaviour differ?

choices.1: All three let the compiler do anything at all
choices.2: UB has no constraints (the optimizer assumes it cannot occur); unspecified is one of several valid outcomes (undocumented which); implementation-defined is a documented choice (e.g. sizeof(int))
choices.3: UB is a documented choice while implementation-defined is unconstrained
choices.4: They are three names for the same concept

question: Why can a written null check disappear, as in: int x = *p; if (p == nullptr) return -1;?

choices.1: p is moved-from after the dereference, so the comparison is unspecified
choices.2: The compiler caches *p and reuses the result for the comparison
choices.3: if statements on pointers are always optimized out
choices.4: Dereferencing *p is UB if p is null, so the compiler assumes p is non-null afterward, making the check provably false dead code that it deletes

question: Which is true of integer overflow in C++?

choices.1: Both signed and unsigned overflow wrap and are defined
choices.2: Both signed and unsigned overflow are UB
choices.3: Signed integer overflow is UB (the optimizer assumes it never happens); unsigned overflow is well-defined and wraps modulo 2^n
choices.4: Unsigned overflow is UB; signed overflow wraps

question: What is the strict aliasing rule, and how do you legally type-pun?

choices.1: Only unions allow any aliasing; memcpy is UB
choices.2: You may freely reinterpret_cast between any pointer types
choices.3: Strict aliasing only applies to floating-point types
choices.4: You may not access an object through a pointer/reference of an unrelated type (char/std::byte may alias anything); pun via std::memcpy or std::bit_cast, not reinterpret_cast

question: Why is reading or writing a T through a misaligned pointer undefined behaviour even on x86?

choices.1: The hardware automatically realigns, so it is merely slow
choices.2: It is always fine on x86; the note overstates the risk
choices.3: Misaligned access is only UB for char buffers
choices.4: Alignment is a precondition the abstract machine assumes; the compiler may emit alignment-requiring instructions (aligned SIMD moves, atomics) that fault or silently tear, and may exploit the known-zero low address bits — the portable fix is memcpy into a properly-aligned object

question: Which of these is NOT undefined behaviour?

choices.1: Two threads writing the same variable with no synchronization
choices.2: Calling v.at(5) on a 3-element vector (it throws std::out_of_range)
choices.3: Reading v[5] on a 3-element vector
choices.4: Reading an uninitialized int's value

question: What are the four MESI cache-line states?

choices.1: Mapped, Evicted, Stale, Idle
choices.2: Modified, Exclusive, Shared, and Invalid
choices.3: Memory, Execute, Store, Index
choices.4: Master, Echo, Slave, Idle

question: What characterizes the Modified (M) state?

choices.1: The line is not valid in this cache
choices.2: The line is clean and may be present in several caches
choices.3: The line is valid only in this cache and is dirty (differs from main memory); it must be written back before eviction
choices.4: The line matches memory and is exclusive to this cache

question: What does the Exclusive (E) state allow that Shared (S) does not?

choices.1: E means the clean line is in this cache only, so it can be upgraded to Modified on a write WITHOUT notifying other caches (a silent upgrade)
choices.2: E means the line is dirty and must be written back
choices.3: E forbids reading the line
choices.4: E allows the line to exist in multiple caches simultaneously

question: What happens when a core writes to a line it holds in Shared (or that is Invalid)?

choices.1: It writes to main memory directly, bypassing the cache
choices.2: It silently upgrades the line to Modified with no bus traffic
choices.3: It issues a BusRdX (read-for-exclusive); other caches invalidate their copies (an M holder writes back first), then the requester loads the line and marks it Modified
choices.4: It downgrades all other copies to Exclusive

question: Why does the E -> M transition on a write minimize bus traffic?

choices.1: Because it evicts the line first
choices.2: Because the line is already exclusive to this cache, the write is a silent upgrade that doesn't need to broadcast invalidations to other caches
choices.3: Because it broadcasts to all caches in one cycle
choices.4: Because it writes through to memory, avoiding snoops

question: What key invariant does MESI guarantee for correctness?

choices.1: At most one cache can hold a given line in the Modified state at a time
choices.2: No line may ever be in the Exclusive state
choices.3: Every line must be Shared across all caches
choices.4: Every write must go to main memory immediately

question: How is a memory address split for a cache lookup?

choices.1: [index | offset | tag], where the tag is the lowest bits
choices.2: [tag | set index | block offset]: the offset selects the byte within a line, the index selects which set to search, and the tag identifies whether the line in that set is the wanted one
choices.3: A single hash of the full address
choices.4: [block offset | set index | tag], in that order from high to low bits

question: Which property of address translation makes VIPT caches possible?

choices.1: Translation reverses the bit order of the address
choices.2: The TLB rewrites the page offset to match the cache index
choices.3: The page offset bits are unchanged by translation (VPN->PFN), so if the cache index comes from within the page offset, the virtual index equals the physical index
choices.4: Virtual and physical tags are always identical

question: What is the trade-off of a PIPT (physically indexed, physically tagged) cache?

choices.1: It avoids synonym and homonym problems and eases coherence, but the CPU must wait for the TLB translation before indexing, adding latency (so it suits L2/L3 more than fast L1)
choices.2: It cannot be used with set-associativity
choices.3: It suffers from both synonyms and homonyms
choices.4: It is the fastest possible L1 design with no downsides

question: What two problems does a VIVT (virtually indexed, virtually tagged) cache suffer?

choices.1: Write-back stalls and false sharing
choices.2: Homonyms (the same virtual address maps to different physical addresses in different processes) and synonyms/aliases (different virtual addresses map to the same physical address)
choices.3: Excessive TLB latency on every access
choices.4: Only cold misses and capacity misses

question: Why is VIPT (virtually indexed, physically tagged) a common L1 design?

choices.1: The virtual index lets the cache start reading a set while the TLB translates in parallel, and the physical tag preserves correctness — overlapping cache access with translation
choices.2: It eliminates all tag comparisons
choices.3: It uses only virtual addresses, so the TLB is never consulted
choices.4: It is the only design compatible with write-through

question: What is the VIPT size constraint for simple, safe operation?

choices.1: number of sets x line size <= page size (i.e. index bits + offset bits <= page-offset bits), which is why 32 KiB 8-way L1 caches with 64-byte lines and 4 KiB pages are common
choices.2: cache size must be a power of two larger than the page size
choices.3: the tag must be larger than the index
choices.4: associativity must equal the number of sets

question: What distinguishes a homonym from a synonym in cache aliasing?

choices.1: A homonym only occurs in PIPT caches
choices.2: They are the same problem under different names
choices.3: A homonym is the same virtual address meaning different physical addresses (across processes); a synonym is different virtual addresses mapping to the same physical address (shared memory)
choices.4: A homonym is two physical addresses; a synonym is two tags

question: How does a fully associative cache differ from a direct-mapped one?

choices.1: Both compare the tag against a single line
choices.2: Fully associative has one location per block; direct-mapped has many
choices.3: Fully associative has no set index — any block can go anywhere and the tag is compared against every line (used for small structures like TLBs); direct-mapped (associativity 1) has exactly one location per block, simple but prone to conflict misses
choices.4: Direct-mapped compares all lines in parallel

question: What are the three stages of the basic fetch-decode-execute cycle?

choices.1: Fetch (get the next instruction from memory/I-cache into the instruction register), decode (interpret the opcode and operands), and execute (perform the operation, then write back)
choices.2: Read, hash, and store
choices.3: Compile, assemble, and execute
choices.4: Load, link, and run

question: What is the role of the Program Counter (PC)?

choices.1: It stores the result of the last ALU operation
choices.2: It counts how many instructions have retired
choices.3: It holds the opcode currently being decoded
choices.4: It holds the address of the next instruction; after a fetch it is incremented to the next instruction, unless a jump/branch modifies it

question: What happens in the decode stage?

choices.1: The result is written back to a register
choices.2: The control unit interprets the opcode, identifies the operation and operands (registers/memory/immediates), and generates the control signals to drive the ALU, registers, and memory
choices.3: The ALU performs the arithmetic for the instruction
choices.4: The next instruction is fetched from memory

question: What kinds of work happen in the execute stage?

choices.1: Only fetching the next instruction
choices.2: Only interpreting the opcode
choices.3: Only incrementing the program counter
choices.4: ALU arithmetic/logic, memory load/store via the data bus, control-flow PC updates for jumps/branches, and writing results back to registers or memory

question: Why does branch prediction exist?

choices.1: At fetch time the CPU does not yet know whether a branch is taken or where it goes; without a prediction the pipeline would stall until the branch resolves, wasting cycles
choices.2: To eliminate the need for a program counter
choices.3: To guarantee branches are always taken
choices.4: To reduce the number of instructions in the program

question: What two predictions must a branch predictor make?

choices.1: Latency and throughput
choices.2: Cache hit and TLB hit
choices.3: Opcode and operand
choices.4: Direction (taken vs not-taken) and target (if taken, which address to fetch next)

question: Why does a 2-bit saturating counter predict loops better than a 1-bit predictor?

choices.1: It requires two consecutive mispredictions to flip its prediction, so a single loop-exit misprediction doesn't change the steady 'taken' prediction the way a 1-bit predictor would
choices.2: It uses the global history of all branches
choices.3: It stores the exact loop trip count
choices.4: It predicts every branch as not-taken

question: What does the Branch Target Buffer (BTB) do?

choices.1: It records the taken/not-taken direction only
choices.2: It rolls back mispredicted instructions
choices.3: It caches a mapping from branch instruction addresses to their predicted target addresses, so the next fetch address is ready before the branch resolves
choices.4: It stores return addresses for function calls

question: What is the cost of a branch misprediction on a modern CPU?

choices.1: Roughly one cycle, since the predictor corrects itself instantly
choices.2: The speculative instructions after the branch are invalidated, the pipeline is flushed and the correct path refetched, and the predictor is updated — costing about 10-20+ cycles
choices.3: Hundreds of cycles, the same as a DRAM access
choices.4: Nothing, because speculation is free

question: What is the core principle of out-of-order (OOO) execution?

choices.1: Execute instructions as soon as their operands are ready (not necessarily in program order), but commit/retire results in program order to preserve correctness
choices.2: Execute and commit all instructions strictly in program order
choices.3: Execute instructions in random order, including commit
choices.4: Run every instruction twice and compare results

question: What problem does register renaming (via the Register Alias Table) solve in an OOO CPU?

choices.1: It increases the clock frequency
choices.2: It enforces in-order memory access
choices.3: It maps architectural registers to physical registers to eliminate false (WAW/WAR) dependencies, exposing more instruction-level parallelism
choices.4: It predicts branch directions

question: What is the purpose of the Reorder Buffer (ROB)?

choices.1: It stores the L1 cache tags
choices.2: It predicts branch targets
choices.3: It renames architectural registers
choices.4: It tracks in-flight instructions in program order and holds their results until they can commit safely, so out-of-order execution still retires in order

question: Why do CPUs split L1 into separate instruction and data caches?

choices.1: Because instructions and data use different address spaces
choices.2: So an instruction fetch and a data load/store can happen in the same cycle without contending for one cache port (avoiding a structural hazard), and so instruction and data accesses don't evict each other
choices.3: Because data caches cannot be pipelined
choices.4: To make the cache larger overall

question: What is the difference between temporal and spatial locality?

choices.1: Temporal locality: a recently accessed location is likely accessed again soon; spatial locality: nearby addresses are likely accessed soon (which is why a whole cache line is fetched on a miss)
choices.2: Temporal locality is about nearby addresses; spatial locality is about reuse over time
choices.3: Both describe reusing the same address
choices.4: Temporal is for instructions and spatial is for data only

question: What are the three classic categories of cache misses?

choices.1: Read, write, and execute misses
choices.2: Hit, near-hit, and far-hit misses
choices.3: Compulsory/cold (first access to the data), capacity (cache too small for the working set), and conflict (multiple addresses map to the same set)
choices.4: L1, L2, and L3 misses

question: What is the difference between write-through and write-back caches?

choices.1: Write-through delays memory updates; write-back updates memory immediately
choices.2: Write-through never touches memory; write-back never touches the cache
choices.3: They differ only in read behaviour
choices.4: Write-through writes to both the cache and memory on each write; write-back writes only to the cache and updates memory later when the line is evicted

question: What is the trade-off between inclusive and exclusive cache hierarchies?

choices.1: Inclusive maximizes capacity; exclusive simplifies coherence
choices.2: Inclusive (L3 superset of L2 superset of L1) simplifies multicore coherence — you only check the shared LLC — but wastes capacity by duplicating data; exclusive maximizes total capacity (no duplication) but is more complex, needing a swap when L1 misses and L2 hits
choices.3: Exclusive caches cannot be used in multicore systems
choices.4: Both store identical data in every level

question: What does 'critical word first' (or early restart) optimize?

choices.1: It evicts the least-recently-used line before fetching
choices.2: It sends the specific requested word to the CPU first (or as soon as it arrives) rather than waiting for the whole cache line, reducing perceived latency while the rest of the line is fetched in the background
choices.3: It writes the modified word back to memory first
choices.4: It fetches the entire line before forwarding any word

question: What are the five stages of the classic RISC pipeline?

choices.1: Fetch, hash, sort, merge, store
choices.2: IF (instruction fetch), ID (instruction decode / register read), EX (execute), MEM (memory access), WB (write back)
choices.3: Load, link, run, commit, retire
choices.4: Decode, rename, dispatch, issue, commit

question: How does pipelining change latency and throughput versus an unpipelined CPU?

choices.1: Per-instruction latency stays the same (e.g. 5 cycles), but throughput rises to about one instruction per cycle after the pipeline fills, because stages of different instructions overlap
choices.2: Both latency and throughput drop to one cycle
choices.3: Latency improves to one cycle but throughput is unchanged
choices.4: Throughput is unchanged but latency improves 5x

question: How does a superscalar CPU differ from a purely pipelined one?

choices.1: Superscalar issues one instruction per cycle while pipelined issues five
choices.2: Superscalar removes the need for a decode stage
choices.3: They are identical; superscalar is just a marketing term
choices.4: Pipelining overlaps the stages of different instructions over time; a superscalar CPU duplicates execution units so it can issue and execute two or more independent instructions in the same cycle

question: What is the purpose of pipeline registers (IF/ID, ID/EX, etc.)?

choices.1: They store the final results of every instruction
choices.2: They rename architectural registers
choices.3: They sit between stages and hold the intermediate data and control signals so each stage can work on a different instruction simultaneously
choices.4: They predict branch directions

question: What are the three classes of pipeline hazards and their typical solutions?

choices.1: Data hazards (an instruction needs a prior result -> forwarding/stalling), control hazards (branches change flow -> branch prediction/delayed branching), and structural hazards (shared hardware resources)
choices.2: Cold, capacity, and conflict hazards
choices.3: Read, write, and execute hazards
choices.4: Local, global, and tournament hazards

question: What fundamentally limits how much a superscalar CPU can speed up a program?

choices.1: The clock frequency alone
choices.2: The size of the L1 instruction cache only
choices.3: The number of pipeline stages
choices.4: Instruction-level parallelism (ILP) — programs often lack enough independent instructions — plus dependency-checking hardware whose complexity grows quadratically with issue width

question: What does prefetching achieve?

choices.1: It evicts unused lines to free cache space
choices.2: It writes dirty lines back to memory early
choices.3: It compresses cache lines to fit more data
choices.4: It fetches data into cache before the CPU needs it, hiding memory latency (DRAM ~100-300 cycles) so the pipeline keeps running instead of stalling on a load

question: What is the difference between software and hardware prefetching?

choices.1: Software prefetching has the programmer/compiler insert explicit prefetch hints (e.g. __builtin_prefetch) — tunable but architecture-specific; hardware prefetching has the CPU automatically detect access patterns with no code changes
choices.2: They are the same mechanism with different names
choices.3: Software prefetching is done by the CPU; hardware prefetching by the compiler
choices.4: Software prefetching never works for arrays; hardware prefetching only works for arrays

question: Which is a real type of hardware prefetcher?

choices.1: Stride prefetcher — it detects a regular stride (A[i], A[i+4], A[i+8], ...) and prefetches ahead
choices.2: Syscall prefetcher — it preloads kernel handlers
choices.3: Branch prefetcher — it predicts taken/not-taken
choices.4: Register prefetcher — it preloads CPU registers

question: Why do most hardware prefetchers stop at 4 KB page boundaries?

choices.1: Because the cache line size is exactly 4 KB
choices.2: Because 4 KB is the maximum size a prefetcher can read at once
choices.3: Crossing a page boundary could require a TLB lookup or trigger a page fault on an unmapped page, and the prefetcher can't reason about OS/program semantics — so it stops at the current page (large pages let it continue further)
choices.4: Because crossing pages would corrupt the TLB

question: What is the behaviour of a software prefetch to an unmapped address on modern CPUs?

choices.1: It is a compile-time error
choices.2: It always triggers a page fault that crashes the program
choices.3: It maps the page on demand, guaranteeing the data arrives
choices.4: Prefetches are treated as non-faulting hints: they will not cause a page fault but abort silently if the address isn't mapped — safe but possibly ineffective

question: When walking a large multi-page array, why might you add software prefetching or use large pages?

choices.1: Because software prefetching is always faster than hardware
choices.2: Because hardware prefetching corrupts data across pages
choices.3: The hardware prefetcher stops at each 4 KB boundary, so the first access of each new page incurs TLB + cache miss latency; software prefetch across boundaries or large pages helps hide that
choices.4: Because large pages disable the cache

question: What is a CPU core versus a hardware thread (SMT/Hyper-Threading)?

choices.1: A hardware thread is a physical engine; a core is a software abstraction
choices.2: A core is a real physical execution engine (its own ALUs, load/store units, branch prediction, pipeline, L1/L2); a hardware thread is a virtualized execution context within a single core
choices.3: A core runs the OS while a hardware thread runs user code
choices.4: They are identical; the terms are interchangeable

question: With SMT, what does each hardware thread have of its own versus share with the sibling thread?

choices.1: Each has its own ALUs and caches but shares the register set
choices.2: Each has completely separate execution units, like distinct cores
choices.3: They share the register set but have private ALUs
choices.4: Each has its own register set and architectural state (so the OS sees a separate CPU), but they share most of the core's physical execution resources — ALUs, load/store units, pipelines, and caches

question: What is the performance benefit of SMT (Hyper-Threading)?

choices.1: It guarantees each thread runs twice as fast
choices.2: It doubles the number of physical ALUs in the core
choices.3: When one thread stalls (e.g. waiting on memory), the other can use the otherwise-idle execution units, improving overall utilization of the core
choices.4: It eliminates the need for caches

question: What was the Northbridge in the traditional PC chipset?

choices.1: The chip that controlled disks, USB, and audio
choices.2: The CPU's internal branch predictor
choices.3: A coprocessor that executed floating-point instructions
choices.4: One of the two main chipset chips, sitting between the CPU and high-speed components (RAM and GPU) and handling the high-bandwidth, low-latency paths

question: Which subsystems did the Northbridge handle (vs the Southbridge)?

choices.1: Disk controllers, USB, and audio, while the Southbridge handled memory
choices.2: Only the CPU's L3 cache
choices.3: Power management and legacy ISA buses exclusively
choices.4: The memory controller, Front-Side Bus, and graphics (AGP/PCIe) — high-speed paths — while the Southbridge handled slow I/O like disk, USB, audio, and BIOS

question: How did the Northbridge's role evolve in modern CPUs?

choices.1: It was renamed to the Southbridge with no functional change
choices.2: Its functions (memory controller, then PCIe controller) moved on-die — AMD's Athlon 64 first integrated the memory controller, and Nehalem/Ryzen integrate both — so the Northbridge no longer exists, replaced by a System Agent/SoC interconnect, leaving only a PCH (modernized Southbridge)
choices.3: It was split into two separate Northbridges for redundancy
choices.4: It grew larger and now sits outside the motherboard

question: What performance benefit came from integrating the memory controller on-die?

choices.1: It removed the need for DRAM entirely
choices.2: It forced all traffic through a single shared bus
choices.3: Higher memory latency but lower cost
choices.4: Lower memory latency (~40-70 ns down to ~20-40 ns), higher bandwidth, and point-to-point interconnects (Intel QPI / AMD HyperTransport) replacing the shared FSB — improving multi-core/NUMA scalability

question: What is the modern equivalent of the Southbridge?

choices.1: The System Agent inside the CPU
choices.2: The L3 cache
choices.3: The Front-Side Bus
choices.4: The Platform Controller Hub (PCH) — a modernized Southbridge handling USB, SATA, and other I/O

question: What scalability problem with SMP does NUMA address?

choices.1: SMP has no cache coherency protocol
choices.2: SMP requires every CPU to have identical clock speeds
choices.3: SMP cannot run more than one thread per core
choices.4: In SMP all CPUs share one memory bus and memory controller, which becomes a bandwidth bottleneck and raises latency as core count grows

question: What is the core idea of NUMA?

choices.1: Replace DRAM with a single large shared cache
choices.2: Give each CPU/socket its own local memory and memory controller; CPUs can still access all memory, but local access is fast and remote access (over the interconnect) is slower
choices.3: Remove the memory controller and use disk as memory
choices.4: Make all memory equally distant from every CPU

question: Why does NUMA improve aggregate memory bandwidth?

choices.1: Because it compresses all memory traffic
choices.2: Because it serializes all memory requests through one controller
choices.3: Each socket has its own DRAM channels and controller that can be kept busy independently, so total bandwidth scales roughly linearly with the number of sockets — no single global bottleneck
choices.4: Because remote memory access is faster than local

question: In a typical 2-socket NUMA system, how do local and remote memory latencies compare?

choices.1: Remote access is instantaneous via cache coherency
choices.2: Local and remote latencies are identical by design
choices.3: Local DRAM access is faster (e.g. ~80 ns) while remote DRAM access pays an extra interconnect hop (e.g. ~130 ns)
choices.4: Remote access is faster because it uses a dedicated bus

question: What is the main programming trade-off NUMA imposes?

choices.1: NUMA requires rewriting all code in assembly
choices.2: NUMA only works for single-threaded programs
choices.3: The programmer or OS/runtime must be NUMA-aware (bind threads to nodes and allocate local memory); a thread that runs on one node but heavily accesses remote memory will see performance collapse
choices.4: NUMA forbids sharing memory between sockets

question: How does a direct-mapped cache place a memory block?

choices.1: A block can go in any line, requiring a search of all lines
choices.2: A block maps to one set but any way within it
choices.3: Each block maps to exactly one cache line (index = (address/block size) % number of lines), so only one tag is compared — fast and simple but prone to conflict misses
choices.4: Blocks are placed randomly to avoid conflicts

question: What characterizes a fully associative cache?

choices.1: A block can go in any line; there is no index, only a tag, so the controller must search all lines in parallel — no conflict misses (only capacity), but expensive, so it suits small caches like TLBs and victim caches
choices.2: It divides the cache into sets with a few ways each
choices.3: It has the lowest hardware cost of all designs
choices.4: Each block maps to exactly one line

question: How does a set-associative cache work as a compromise?

choices.1: The cache is divided into sets, each with N ways; a block maps to one set (index = (address/block size) % number of sets) but can occupy any way in that set — fewer conflict misses than direct-mapped, cheaper than fully associative
choices.2: Every block can go in any line with no index
choices.3: Each block has exactly one possible location
choices.4: It removes tags entirely and uses only an index

question: In a 4-way set-associative cache, what does '4-way' mean?

choices.1: The cache has exactly 4 sets total
choices.2: Each set has 4 slots (ways) a block can occupy, and a lookup compares 4 tags in parallel within the selected set
choices.3: The cache line is 4 bytes
choices.4: Each block maps to 4 different sets

question: What replacement policies are used when a full set needs an eviction?

choices.1: Write-through and write-back
choices.2: FIFO only, because sets are queues
choices.3: VIPT and PIPT
choices.4: LRU (evict the least recently used line), Random (pick a random way), or PLRU (pseudo-LRU, a cheaper hardware approximation of LRU)

question: How do an SRAM cell and a DRAM cell differ structurally?

choices.1: Both use capacitors but SRAM has more of them
choices.2: SRAM stores each bit in a flip-flop (~6 transistors) that is stable while powered (no refresh); DRAM stores each bit as charge in a capacitor plus an access transistor (1T1C), which leaks and needs periodic refresh
choices.3: SRAM uses a capacitor while DRAM uses a flip-flop
choices.4: SRAM uses magnetic storage; DRAM uses transistors

question: Why is SRAM faster than DRAM?

choices.1: SRAM runs at a higher clock frequency than the CPU
choices.2: DRAM is faster; SRAM is only cheaper
choices.3: SRAM avoids refresh and sense-amplifier delays — its flip-flops hold a stable state read directly — whereas DRAM must sense a leaking charge and then restore it, adding latency
choices.4: SRAM caches results so repeat reads are instant

question: Why does DRAM use multiplexed (row/column) addressing with RAS and CAS?

choices.1: To refresh the capacitors automatically
choices.2: To double the data rate per clock cycle
choices.3: To cut the pin count: addresses are sent as a row part (latched by RAS) then a column part (latched by CAS) over the same pins (e.g. 10 pins instead of 20 for 1M cells), at the cost of extra access steps
choices.4: To make every cell directly addressable with no latency

question: Why is DRAM used for main memory and SRAM for CPU caches?

choices.1: DRAM never needs refreshing while SRAM does
choices.2: SRAM cannot store more than a few bytes
choices.3: DRAM is optimized for density (small cheap leaky capacitors → GBs cheaply) while SRAM is optimized for speed (bigger cells, lower density, higher power, much faster)
choices.4: DRAM is faster but more expensive than SRAM

question: What does DDR (Double Data Rate) memory do compared to SDR?

choices.1: It transfers data twice per clock cycle (on both the rising and falling clock edges), doubling bandwidth without raising the clock frequency
choices.2: It doubles the clock frequency to double bandwidth
choices.3: It transfers data once per cycle but with twice-wide words
choices.4: It removes the need for a memory controller

question: What are typical latency figures for SRAM vs DRAM?

choices.1: SRAM ~50-200 cycles; DRAM ~1-3 cycles
choices.2: Both are roughly 1 cycle
choices.3: Both are roughly 200 cycles
choices.4: SRAM ~1-3 CPU cycles (sub-nanosecond); DRAM ~50-200 cycles (tens of nanoseconds)

question: What is thread (CPU) affinity?

choices.1: Assigning higher priority to a thread
choices.2: Binding a thread or process to a specific core (or set of cores) so it always runs there instead of being migrated by the OS scheduler
choices.3: Splitting one thread across multiple cores
choices.4: Letting the scheduler move a thread freely for load balancing

question: Why does keeping a thread pinned to a core improve performance?

choices.1: It disables interrupts on that core
choices.2: It keeps the core's private L1/L2 caches and TLB state hot and memory accesses local (on NUMA), avoiding cold caches and remote access; migration can cost 10-50% in latency-sensitive workloads
choices.3: It gives the thread exclusive use of the L3 cache
choices.4: It increases the core's clock frequency

question: What is the difference between hard and soft affinity?

choices.1: Hard affinity prefers cores; soft affinity forces them
choices.2: They are two names for the same thing
choices.3: Hard affinity is for I/O threads; soft for CPU threads
choices.4: Hard (pinned) affinity means the thread must run only on the specified cores (for real-time/low-latency work); soft (preferred) affinity means the scheduler prefers certain cores but may migrate if needed

question: On a NUMA system, why combine thread affinity with memory locality?

choices.1: Because affinity automatically migrates memory between nodes
choices.2: Because NUMA nodes share one memory controller anyway
choices.3: Pinning a thread and allocating its memory on the same NUMA node keeps accesses local; otherwise a thread on node 0 constantly reading node 1's DRAM can be up to ~2x slower
choices.4: Because remote memory is faster than local memory

question: How do you set thread affinity on Linux?

choices.1: Linux does not support thread affinity
choices.2: Via pthread_setaffinity_np with a cpu_set_t (CPU_SET to pick cores), or taskset -c from the shell; numactl --cpunodebind/--membind handles NUMA placement
choices.3: Via SetThreadAffinityMask, which is the Linux system call
choices.4: By setting the nice value with renice

question: What is a TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) and why does it exist?

choices.1: A buffer that holds pending memory writes before flushing
choices.2: A small CPU cache of recent virtual-to-physical page translations, used to avoid an expensive page-table walk (which can take hundreds of cycles) on every memory access
choices.3: A queue of TLB misses waiting for the OS
choices.4: A cache of recently executed instructions

question: What does a TLB entry store?

choices.1: The full cache line of data for that page
choices.2: The Virtual Page Number (tag), the Physical Page Number (result), permission bits, an ASID/PCID, plus valid/dirty/accessed bits, page size, and a global bit
choices.3: The page's contents and a checksum
choices.4: Only the virtual address with no metadata

question: How are TLBs typically organized like caches?

choices.1: A single TLB shared by all cores with no levels
choices.2: Instruction and data always share one L1 TLB
choices.3: Split at L1 into an ITLB (instruction-fetch addresses) and a DTLB (data load/store addresses), often unified at L2, with a small/fast L1 TLB and a larger/slower L2 TLB
choices.4: One TLB per page rather than per core

question: What is the difference between a hardware-managed and a software-managed TLB on a miss?

choices.1: Hardware-managed traps to the OS; software-managed walks in hardware
choices.2: Software-managed is faster because it avoids hardware complexity
choices.3: Both always trap to the OS; the difference is only the format
choices.4: Hardware-managed (x86/ARM): a hardware page-table walker automatically walks fixed-format page tables and refills the TLB; software-managed (MIPS/SPARC): a TLB-miss exception traps to an OS handler that refills it, allowing any page-table format but with higher per-miss latency

question: What does an ASID/PCID in a TLB entry accomplish?

choices.1: It records the page's access frequency for LRU
choices.2: It marks the entry as global across all processes
choices.3: It stores the physical frame number
choices.4: It tags entries with process identity so translations from different processes don't collide, avoiding a full TLB flush on every context switch

question: Why do most modern CPUs use hardware-managed rather than software-managed TLBs?

choices.1: Because hardware-managed TLBs offer more page-table format flexibility
choices.2: Because software-managed TLBs cannot support virtual memory
choices.3: Speed: a trap on every miss is too slow unless the TLB is huge or misses are rare; x86/ARM use highly optimized, speculative, multi-level page-table walkers that make hardware translation very fast
choices.4: Because software-managed TLBs require more transistors

question: What is transactional memory?

choices.1: A way to allocate memory that is automatically transactional across processes
choices.2: A mechanism where a block of code runs as if in an atomic transaction — either all of it happens or none of it does — without explicit locking, with automatic rollback on conflict
choices.3: A lock that blocks all other threads during a critical section
choices.4: A persistent memory that survives crashes

question: Why use transactional memory?

choices.1: It guarantees transactions never abort
choices.2: It makes every memory access durable to disk
choices.3: It is always faster than lock-free programming
choices.4: It avoids error-prone manual locking and lets concurrent threads run in parallel without waiting when conflicts are rare, rolling back automatically when they do conflict

question: How do Hardware (HTM) and Software (STM) transactional memory differ?

choices.1: They are identical except for the name
choices.2: HTM uses software logs; STM uses cache tracking
choices.3: HTM is portable across all CPUs; STM requires special hardware
choices.4: HTM has the CPU track the read-set/write-set and commit if no other core conflicts (e.g. Intel TSX/RTM), else discard and restart; STM needs no special CPU support and manages read/write logs in software (e.g. GCC libitm, -fgnu-tm), but is slower

question: How does HTM use the MESI cache-coherence protocol to detect conflicts?

choices.1: The read-set is the cache lines touched in S/E state and the write-set those in M state; if another core writes a line in your read-set or write-set, MESI invalidation aborts the transaction — the coherence hardware is the conflict detector
choices.2: It writes a software bitmap of all touched addresses
choices.3: It disables MESI during the transaction
choices.4: It locks every cache line it touches until commit

question: Why does an HTM transaction abort if it touches too many cache lines?

choices.1: If a line in the read/write-set is evicted from L1, the CPU can no longer verify whether another core modified it, so to preserve atomicity it aborts the transaction
choices.2: Because MESI cannot track more than 8 lines
choices.3: Because the OS forcibly preempts large transactions
choices.4: Because large transactions overflow the instruction cache

question: What is the practical rule for HTM transactions?

choices.1: They work best when many threads update the same memory
choices.2: They should include I/O so the rollback is logged
choices.3: They must be small, quick, and cache-friendly; they degrade badly under frequent conflicts (conflict storms cause retry explosions) and abort on interrupts, page faults, or syscalls/I-O
choices.4: They should touch as much memory as possible to amortize overhead

question: What does ACID stand for?

choices.1: Availability, Concurrency, Isolation, Distribution
choices.2: Access, Control, Isolation, Defense
choices.3: Atomicity, Concurrency, Integrity, Durability
choices.4: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability

question: What does Atomicity guarantee?

choices.1: A transaction happens all-or-nothing — e.g. a transfer cannot subtract from Alice and then fail without adding to Bob; either both steps happen or neither does
choices.2: The database always stays in a valid state per its rules
choices.3: Committed data survives a crash
choices.4: Concurrent transactions never see each other's work

question: What does Isolation control?

choices.1: How much one concurrent transaction can see of another's temporary, unfinished work — e.g. two people must not both book the last flight seat
choices.2: Whether a transaction is all-or-nothing
choices.3: Whether a committed transaction survives power loss
choices.4: Whether replicas eventually converge

question: What does BASE stand for?

choices.1: Balanced, Atomic, Scalable, Efficient
choices.2: Basically Atomic, Strongly Eventual
choices.3: Backup, Availability, Sharding, Eventual
choices.4: Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent

question: What does 'Eventually Consistent' mean in BASE?

choices.1: If no new updates occur, all copies of the data will eventually become consistent — e.g. some users see an old profile picture for a few seconds before everyone sees the new one
choices.2: Data is never allowed to be stale
choices.3: The system rejects writes until all replicas agree
choices.4: Every read immediately reflects the latest write

question: What is the core difference between ACID and BASE?

choices.1: ACID is for distributed NoSQL; BASE is for single-node relational systems
choices.2: ACID is eventually consistent; BASE is strongly consistent
